input: Hello doctor,I think I might have UTI, since I suffer from burning sensation while urinating, and then cloudy urine at the beginning. I did urine examination routine, in which leukocyte esterase was positive, nitrites negative, and high pus cells in urine 8 to 10. Also did urine culture after that and nothing such came in it. I had unprotected vaginal sex four months ago. Is there any chance, that this is not UTI but some STD? Please help me.
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. I have gone through your query and your reports (attachment removed to protect patient identity). I would like to know a few more details to answer your query better: It is unlikely for an STD to present for four months after sexual contact, also your symptoms are more suggestive of a UTI. It is possible to have a negative urine culture because of sampling errors (the urine specimen given may not have had an adequate bacterial load) or organisms that do not grow in culture or infection of the prostate gland.  You would require a detailed clinical examination by a urologist to ascertain the probable cause of UTI and also rule out STD. I would also advise a repeat urine analysis and urine culture and serological tests if you are concerned about the possibility of STD\s from the unprotected sexual contact. In the meantime, you can  ChatDoctor.  For more information consult an urologist online
answer_chatgpt:
Hello,

Based on your symptoms and urine results, it is likely that you have a urinary tract infection (UTI). The positive leukocyte esterase and high pus cells in urine indicate that there is an infection present, although the negative nitrite result suggests that it may not be a severe UTI. The absence of bacteria in the urine culture is not uncommon, as the infection could be caused by non-bacterial organisms.

However, given that you had unprotected vaginal sex four months ago, there is still a chance that this could be a sexually transmitted infection (STI). Some STIs, such as chlamydia and gonorrhea, can cause similar symptoms to UTIs. It is important to get tested for STIs to rule out any other possible infections. It is also important to practice safe sex to prevent future infections.

I recommend that you schedule an appointment with your healthcare provider to discuss your symptoms and get tested for STIs. They can provide you with the appropriate treatment for your infection.
answer_chatdoctor: 1) You need to do a swab test of your vagina to rule out STDs like trichomoniasis.2) The pus cells are suggestive of an infection.3) It could be a UTI as well, but if you have had no other symptoms besides burning during urination, then it is less likely.4) Your urine should be sent for a microscopic examination to look for bacteria or yeast.5) Doing urine cultures will help determine what kind of organisms are causing your problem.6) Get treated with antibiotics.7) If you are having painful urination, you may also need anti-inflammatory medications.

input: Hello doctor,Yesterday, I got my ECG done. It shows sinus rhythm, abnormal left axis deviation, left anterior fascicular block. I am worried. I have long-term anxiety about heart. I am a smoker but not alcoholic. I always have a fear of heart disease. Kindly advice. My BP is 110/70 mmHg, triglycerides is 144 mg/dl and fasting blood sugar is 84 mg/dl.
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. Left anterior fascicular block is always associated with left axis deviation and caused by either hypertension, myocardial infarction or idiopathic fibrosis. This block means that the pathway of electricity of the heart in the left ventricle is blocked which may or may not affect the pumping function of the heart. I suggest that you perform echocardiography to identify the cause and the general function of the heart. Do not smoke as it may cause myocardial infarctions in the future. For more information consult an internal medicine physician online
answer_chatgpt:
Hello! I understand your concern. Firstly, sinus rhythm is the normal rhythm of the heart and is good news. However, your ECG does indicate an abnormal left axis deviation and left anterior fascicular block. These are electrical abnormalities of the heart that can be caused by various factors. Smoking can increase the risk of heart disease, so it would be beneficial to quit smoking.

Your blood pressure, triglycerides, and fasting blood sugar levels are within normal limits, which is good news. However, it's important to have a thorough evaluation by a cardiologist who can investigate further and determine the severity of the abnormalities detected on the ECG. Treatment may be recommended, depending on the findings.

It's also important to address your anxiety about heart disease. Anxiety can have a significant impact on your health and quality of life. Consider seeking help from a mental health professional to manage your anxiety and improve your overall well-being.

In summary, consult with a cardiologist for a thorough evaluation, consider quitting smoking, and address your anxiety with a mental health professional. I hope this helps!
answer_chatdoctor: Hi, Thanks for writing in to us. Your ECG findings do not indicate any significant cardiac pathology. Left Axis Deviation is an electrical conduction defect which occurs when there is a delay in the activation of the right bundle branch compared to the left bundle branch. This can be seen as a result of many conditions including chronic hypertension, hyperthyroidism, certain medications etc. So it is important that we get your blood pressure checked along with other tests to see if this is the cause for your ECG changes. The other finding of anterior fascicular block may also be due to many causes like cardiomyopathies, congestive heart failure, valvular heart diseases etc. Again, these need to be investigated. I would advise you to consult your cardiologist who will examine you and order appropriate tests depending upon your clinical findings. Hope this helps

input: Hello doctor,I woke up suddenly at 1\o clock in the night to urinate. I have a burning sensation after that. I woke up once an hour until about 5 am and having the same problem. There was only little urination, and the burning sensation started to decrease after each time I urinate. At around 4 am, I noticed light pink spotting on the toilet paper after the first wipe. It has occurred all day and at 6 pm the burning has almost completely disappeared. I am due for my period in seven days and I had unprotected sex eight and 13 days ago. A little concerned, I had UTI in the past and never had any spotting. I have googled, possible implantation bleeding, and took an EPT two days ago, and it turned out negative. I want a little more possible explanation.
answer_icliniq: Hello, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. This can well be an implantation bleed considering you have had unprotected sex recently unless proven otherwise. The EPT (early pregnancy test) test is not sensitive enough during the cycle at all, and can only detect the hCG levels on the seventh day post missed period. The pregnancy can be rather confirmed with a blood serum beta hCG level, which if found to be raised indicates pregnancy. If this test is negative and pregnancy is ruled out, then the spotting can be attributed to breakthrough bleeding due to lower levels of progesterone immediately after delayed ovulation as in this case possibly causing a small scanty with ChatDoctor.  You need to furnish details about your LMP (last menstrual period) and regularity of cycles to comment further.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello,

Based on your symptoms, it is possible that you may have a urinary tract infection (UTI). UTIs are caused by bacteria infecting the urinary tract and can cause symptoms such as burning during urination, frequent urination, and spotting. It is also possible that the spotting you experienced could be related to your menstrual cycle or other gynecological issues.

However, it is also important to note that implantation bleeding is a possible explanation for the spotting. Implantation bleeding occurs when the fertilized egg implants into the wall of the uterus, and can cause light spotting or bleeding.

Since you had unprotected sex in the past few days, it is also possible that you may be experiencing early pregnancy symptoms. Although the negative EPT result may be an indication that you are not pregnant, it is still possible that you may be experiencing very early pregnancy symptoms.

To determine the cause of your symptoms, it is recommended that you schedule an appointment with your healthcare provider. They can perform a urine test to check for a UTI and discuss other possible causes for your symptoms.

input: Hello doctor,The attached PDF are the results from CBC tests and Hb electrophoresis of a girl baby. The first CBC was done in one month, and the following CBC and Hb electrophoresis tests were done in two years of age. The tests seem to indicate that the girl is (at least) a carrier of the alpha thalassemia Hb constant spring (CS) mutation. I have two questions related to this.Is it possible to say from the test results which of the following Hb genotypes are possible?a)\t3 normal, 1 CS.b)\t2 normal, 1 CS, 1 deletion.c)\t2 normal, 2 CS.d)\tSomething else?What is the difference between a, b and c above in terms of expected symptoms later in life?Additional information: weight, height, and head circumference are normal. (14 kg, 88 cm, and 51 cm). She is taking Budesonide 200 ug and Montek 5 mg once per day.
answer_icliniq: Hello,  Welcome to ChatDoctor. Hemoglobin constant spring is non-deletional alpha thalassemia. This abnormal hemoglobin results from point mutation at the stop codon of the \xce\xb12-globin gene (TAA > CAA) which leads to the addition of 31 amino acids to a normal \xce\xb1-globin sequence. The heterozygous of Hb CS (hemoglobin constant spring) is clinically and hematologically normal with mild anemia in some cases as it appears to be in this case. Heterozygous cases have a good prognosis and a fairly normal life expectancy. The genetic abnormality can only be confirmed by genetic testing.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello! Thank you for providing me with the test results of the CBC tests and Hb electrophoresis of a girl baby. Based on the tests, it does indicate that the girl is a carrier of the alpha thalassemia Hb constant spring (CS) mutation. 

To answer your first question, yes, it is possible to determine the possible Hb genotypes from the test results. The Hemoglobin genotype for alpha thalassemia can be deduced as follows: 

- If all four alpha chains are present and normal, the genotype is written as αα/αα.
- If one of the four alpha chains is missing, the genotype is written as αα/α-.
- If two of the four alpha chains are missing, the genotype is written as α-/α-.
- If three of the four alpha chains are missing, the genotype is lethal.

Given that the girl is a carrier of the alpha thalassemia Hb constant spring (CS) mutation, it is possible that the Hb genotype can be deduced as the following:

- a) 3 normal, 1 CS - this would indicate that the girl has three normal alpha genes and one alpha constant spring gene.
- b) 2 normal, 1 CS, 1 deletion - this would indicate that the girl has two normal alpha genes, one alpha constant spring gene, and one deleted alpha gene.
- c) 2 normal, 2 CS - this would indicate that the girl has two alpha constant spring genes and two normal alpha genes.

To answer your second question, the difference between these possible genotypes is that those with more alpha constant spring genes are at a higher risk of developing mild anemia and may require occasional blood transfusions. However, in all three cases, the baby may not show any symptoms early in life. 

It is important to note that in addition to the hemoglobinopathies, the girl's symptoms and treatment may also be influenced by the presence of the other drug she is taking. Therefore, it is important that her pediatrician monitors her condition regularly and makes the necessary adjustments to any medication or treatment plan.
answer_chatdoctor: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. As you describe here, the child has been found to be a carrier for the alpha-thalassemia Hb constant spring (CS) mutation. The child can not develop any problem because of this mutation as long as she takes care of her dietary intake of iron. The child should avoid taking food containing high amount of iron like spinach, meat, eggs, etc. The child has no other health problems, so there is nothing to worry about. Hope this answers your query. Wishing good health to your daughter.

input: Hi doctor,What is Rhesus? My wife is pregnant and started clinic today.
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. At first visit, the pregnant lady\s blood group needs to be checked. There are many blood groups like A, B, O, AB and another thing which we need to know is rhesus positive or rhesus negative. So on the basis of rhesus, it is A+, A-, B+, B-, O+, O-, AB+, AB -. So, every blood group have rhesus classification like rhesus positive and rhesus negative. A positive and a negative.  Rhesus has to be known as there is one issue of rhesus incompatibility. If both mother and father of the child are rhesus positive or both rhesus negative, then no issue. But, if mother is rhesus negative and father is rhesus positive and developing baby is rhesus positive, then mother can develop antibodies against fetal blood that can cause lysis of blood cells most likely in next pregnancy which can lead to anemic baby. Rh negative mothers with Rh positive husbands need anti-D injections. They need to have twice at 28 and 32 weeks of gestation without any cause but in case of bleeding during pregnancy also need injection, after birth also. Check your blood group as well if wife is rhesus negative. She should have regular antenatal visits and in case of bleeding or pain report to gynecology emergency.  If wife is Rh negative, have blood grouping of husband. Regular antenatal check-ups in maternal medicine unit if Rh negative and some other complications are related to it. Anti-D injections at 28 and 32 weeks. Balanced diet, weight, and BMI, folic acid. Regular antenatal check-ups. Report in case of any bleeding.
answer_chatgpt: Hi there! Rhesus, also known as Rh factor, is a protein that is found on the surface of red blood cells. It is important to determine an individual's Rh status, as a person who is Rh-positive has this protein on their red blood cells, while a person who is Rh-negative does not. This information is important during pregnancy because if the mother is Rh-negative and the baby is Rh-positive, there is a risk that the mother's immune system will recognize the baby's Rh-positive blood cells as foreign and mount an immune response against them, which can lead to complications such as hemolytic disease of the newborn. Your wife's clinic will likely test her blood to determine her Rh status and monitor her pregnancy accordingly. Congratulations on your growing family and good luck!
answer_chatdoctor: Hi, I am Chat Doctor.  I have read your question and understand your concerns. Let me try to help you Question : What is Rhesus? Answer :Rhesus (Rh) factor is an inherited blood trait that affects how your body responds to certain diseases. It is one of the most common inherited factors in humans. It is also known as the Rhesus Factor or RH factor. The Rh factor is determined by whether or not you have inherited rhesus positive antigens from your mother. The Rh factor is usually detected through a simple blood test called the Rh factor test. Rhesus negative individuals do not have these antigens and therefore can receive Rh positive blood without any problems. Rhesus positive individuals may develop antibodies against Rh-positive red blood cells, which can cause problems for subsequent pregnancies. The Rh factor is an important factor when considering blood transfusion. Rh-negative

Based on your description, it sounds like you may have an infection in the area of your wisdom tooth and failed root canal. The white rings on the roof of your mouth could also be an indication of infection. Here are some tips to help alleviate your pain and discomfort until you see your dentist:

1. Rinse your mouth with warm salt water. This can help reduce inflammation and kill bacteria.

2. Use a cold compress to reduce swelling. Wrap a bag of ice or frozen vegetables in a towel and hold it against your cheek on the affected side for 10-15 minutes at a time.

3. Take over-the-counter pain relief medications like ibuprofen or acetaminophen as directed on the packaging.

4. Avoid eating on the affected side of your mouth and stick to soft foods that won't aggravate the area.

5. Keep your mouth clean by brushing and flossing regularly, but be gentle around the area of the infected tooth.

6. Try using a numbing gel or oral analgesic to ease the pain. You can get these at most drugstores.

It's important that you see your dentist as soon as possible to address the underlying issue causing your pain and infection. They may recommend antibiotics or other treatments to help clear up the infection and alleviate your symptoms.

input: Hi doctor,What are the physical findings seen in breast cancer? Please explain about the pathophysiology observed in breast cancer.
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. These are the physical findings found in breast cancer. There can be a painless lump or a change in breast size, skin changes, nipple inversion, nipple abnormalities or discharge, and axillary lump. The pathophysiology shows damage to DNA, and genetic changes lead to breast cancer. It is also linked to other factors such as estrogen exposure, smoking, age and family history. Some individuals inherit defects in BRCA1, BRCA2, PTEN (phosphatase and tensin homolog) and P53 (tumor protein). People having a family history of ovarian or breast cancer can have an increased risk of getting breast cancer.
answer_chatgpt:
Physical findings in breast cancer can vary depending on the stage of the disease, as well as the location and size of the tumor. Some common physical signs of breast cancer include:

1. Lump or thickening in the breast or underarm area
2. Change in breast size or shape
3. Dimpling or puckering of the skin on the breast
4. Nipple inversion or retraction
5. Skin rash or redness on the breast
6. Nipple discharge other than breast milk

The pathophysiology of breast cancer involves the abnormal growth and proliferation of cells in the breast tissue. Normally, breast cells grow and divide in a regulated manner, but in breast cancer, this regulation is disrupted. Cancerous cells can form a tumor, which can grow and invade nearby tissues and even spread to other parts of the body.

There are several types of breast cancer, including ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC), and invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC). DCIS is the earliest form of breast cancer, in which the abnormal cells are confined to the milk ducts. IDC and ILC are more advanced forms of breast cancer, in which the abnormal cells have invaded other parts of the breast tissue. 

The exact cause of breast cancer is not known, but there are several risk factors associated with the development of the disease, including age, gender, family history, and exposure to certain environmental factors. Early detection and treatment of breast cancer can improve the prognosis and increase the chances of successful treatment.
answer_chatdoctor: The most common symptom of breast cancer is a lump or swelling in the breast that can be felt. Other signs and symptoms include: a change in the size or shape of your breast; skin changes such as dimpling or puckering; nipple discharge (especially if it is bloody); pain or tenderness in the breast; thickening of the skin of the breast; redness or scaliness of the nipples; a rash around the nipples; or a sore on the nipple or breast. Breast cancer may also cause a lump or swelling in other parts of the body, such as the armpit or neck. It may also spread to the bones or liver.

input: Hello doctor,Actually, we have a newly born baby boy of around one month and 15 days. Our baby has a problem that, he continuously cries. Hello, I understand your concern about your baby. It is normal for newborns to cry as it is their way of communicating with their parents, but excessive crying can be a sign of discomfort or pain. Here are some possible reasons for your baby's continuous crying:

- Hunger: Newborns eat frequently and may cry when hungry. Be sure to feed your baby on demand or every 2-3 hours.

- Colic: Colic is a common condition among newborns that causes intense crying spells, usually occurring in the late afternoon or evening. It is not clear what causes colic, but it usually resolves by 3-4 months of age. Try using some soothing techniques, such as swaddling, gentle rocking, or white noise.

- Gas or digestive issues: Babies may cry when they have gas or digestive issues. Burping your baby after each feeding and gently massaging the belly can help relieve discomfort.

- Overstimulation: Newborns can become overwhelmed by too much stimulation, such as loud noises or bright lights. Calming activities such as giving a warm bath or playing soft music may soothe your baby.

If you are concerned about your baby's crying, or if he seems to be in pain or distress, you should contact your pediatrician. They can perform a physical exam and evaluate your baby to determine if there are any underlying medical issues.

1) Bonding: This is a process where a tooth-colored resin material is applied to the chipped area and then shaped and polished to match the rest of the tooth. This is a relatively quick and inexpensive procedure, perfect for small chips.

2) Veneers: If bonding isn't an option, a veneer may be required. Veneers are thin, custom-made shells that cover the front surface of a tooth, providing a natural look.

3) Crown: For more severe chips or to protect the tooth, a crown may be recommended. A crown is a cap that completely covers the damaged tooth, providing an additional layer of protection.

Your dentist will examine the chipped tooth and recommend the best treatment option for you.

input: Hello doctor,I am 30 male. I am suffering from central diabetes insipidus for the last three years. My all body, skin, and mucous membranes like penis glans, nose, nostrils and eyes have extreme dryness and frequent nightfall 4 to 5 times in a week. Please advice medicine. currently, I am taking Desmopressin.
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. Central diabetes insipidus is a very rare case of diabetes. In this condition blood sugar doses are normal but due to excessive urination, the body suffers from electrolyte imbalances which lead to excessive thirst,  ChatDoctor.  It is due to lack or very less secretion of ADH hormones. ADH, vasopressin. The treatment plan would be to suppress extra urination and need to reduce
answer_chatgpt:
Dear patient, 
Central diabetes insipidus is a rare condition that occurs when your body doesn't produce enough antidiuretic hormone (ADH). This hormone helps regulate the amount of fluid in your body. Without enough ADH, your kidneys can't properly balance your body's fluid levels. This can lead to many symptoms, including excessive thirst and urination. 

Desmopressin is a medication commonly used to treat central diabetes insipidus by mimicking the effects of ADH. However, it may not address all of your symptoms. The dryness of your skin, mucous membranes, and frequent nightfall could be related to your condition or other underlying factors. 

I recommend consulting with an endocrinologist, who can evaluate your condition and prescribe appropriate treatment. They may also refer you to a dermatologist for further examination of your skin and mucous membranes. Additionally, practicing good hydration habits and using moisturizing products may offer some relief for your symptoms. 

Please do not hesitate to seek professional medical advice on this matter. I hope this helps.
answer_chatdoctor: Hi, Thanks for writing to us. Diabetes insipidus is due to deficiency of antidiuretic hormone (ADH). It can be due to genetic defect or damage to hypothalamus region of brain. The symptoms that you have mentioned are suggestive of same. Desmopressin is the treatment of choice. But it has side effects like increased thirst, polyuria and nocturia. I would advise you to take tablets like acetazolamide which will increase water absorption by kidney. This will reduce the urination frequency and also help in treating the dehydration. You may need to monitor your blood electrolytes while on this medication. Hope this helps.

Hello! Ovral L is a combination hormonal contraceptive pill used for birth control. If taken within 24-72 hours of unprotected intercourse, Ovral L is highly effective (95-98%) in preventing pregnancy. It works by inhibiting ovulation and thickening cervical mucus to prevent sperm from entering the uterus. Therefore, taking one pill of Ovral L within 24 hours of intercourse as you did can significantly reduce the risk of pregnancy. 

However, taking two Ovral L pills in one day is not recommended and may cause side effects such as nausea, vomiting, bloating, breast tenderness, and irregular bleeding. It is essential to remember that Ovral L is meant to be taken as indicated, and taking extra pills may not increase your protection against pregnancy.

If you are worried about being pregnant, you can take a pregnancy test after two weeks of the intercourse, or you can consult a healthcare provider for more information about emergency contraception options. In addition, using another form of contraceptive method such as condoms, diaphragms, or intrauterine devices (IUDs) along with hormonal pills can provide extra protection against pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections.

Overall, it is essential to follow the recommended dosages of Ovral L or any hormonal contraceptive pill for birth control, as taking too many or too few pills may reduce its effectiveness and cause unwanted side effects.
answer_chatdoctor: 1) Ovral L is an emergency contraceptive pill which works by delaying ovulation and thus preventing fertilization. It contains high doses of progesterone and estrogen hormones which can cause irregular bleeding, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, abdominal pain, breast tenderness etc. But there is no evidence that it causes abortion. So it should be taken as early as possible in case of unprotected intercourse. You may take one more tablet after 12 hours if the first one does not work. 2) I-Pill is also an emergency contraceptive pill containing high dose of progesterone hormone which works by preventing implantation of egg in the uterine wall instead of delaying ovulation. Thus it does not cause abortion. 3) Unwanted 72 is another brand name for Chat Doctor.

input: Hello doctor,Are there chances of developing chickenpox after shingles?
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. Chickenpox and shingles are caused due to the same virus. Chickenpox occurs first and the virus stays dormant in the dorsal ganglia of the spinal nerves. After years or when immunity decreases, the person gets an attack of shingles. Most people get shingles only once, but few can get 2 to 3 times. After shingles no one gets chickenpox.
answer_chatgpt: As an AI language model, I cannot be a doctor and provide medical advice to patients. However, I can inform you that shingles is caused by the reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus (VZV) that causes chickenpox. If you have had chickenpox before, the virus remains dormant in your body and can reactivate later in life, causing shingles. It is possible to develop chickenpox after shingles if you have not had chickenpox previously or if you have a weakened immune system. If you have symptoms of chickenpox or shingles, it is important to consult with a healthcare provider for an accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment.
answer_chatdoctor: 100% yes. Shingles is caused by varicella zoster virus (VZV) which is also responsible for causing Chicken pox. So if someone has had chickenpox in their past they can develop shingles again. There are two types of shingles: herpes zoster and postherpetic neuralgia. Herpes zoster is an acute viral infection that usually lasts 2-3 weeks. Postherpetic neuralgia is a chronic pain condition that occurs after healing of the skin lesions. It is estimated that approximately 15 to 20 percent of people with shingles will develop postherpetic neuralgia.

answer_chatgpt:
Hello,

It sounds like you may be experiencing tartar buildup. Tartar, also known as calculus, is a hard, yellowish substance that forms on teeth and is caused by bacteria and plaque buildup over time. Tartar cannot be removed with regular brushing and flossing and usually requires professional dental cleaning.

In some cases, certain factors such as genetics, smoking, diet, and poor oral hygiene can increase the risk of tartar buildup.

To prevent or reduce tartar buildup, it is important to maintain good oral hygiene practices such as:

- Brushing twice a day with fluoride toothpaste
- Flossing daily
- Using an antiseptic mouthwash
- Eating a balanced diet and limiting sugary or starchy foods
- Avoiding tobacco products.

In addition to good oral hygiene practices, regular dental checkups and professional cleanings can help prevent and manage tartar buildup.

If your tartar buildup persists despite your efforts, it may be helpful to talk to your dentist or dental hygienist for additional advice and treatment options.

Secondly, it could also be due to a condition called Gingivitis. Gingivitis occurs when the gums become inflamed from plaque build up. This can lead to bad breath, redness and swelling of the gums, receding gum line and loosening of teeth.3. I suggest you visit your dentist and get evaluated and treated accordingly. Hope I have answered your query. Let me know if I can assist you further.

The liver transplantation is the only definite therapy for advanced liver scarring such as in your\s father case. However, it may take some time for the medications to show results, and patience and persistence are essential.

Fluid buildup in the abdomen, a condition called ascites, is a common complication of chronic liver disease. Diuretics such as Lasilactone are prescribed to reduce fluid buildup in the body. However, if the ascites persists despite medication, tapping or draining the fluid from the abdomen may be necessary.

Back pain may be caused by a number of factors, including muscle strain or inflammation of the liver. Painkillers such as Lket can help relieve the pain, but it's important to identify the cause of the pain and address it directly if possible.

Low blood pressure can be a side effect of some of the medications your father is taking, particularly diuretics. It's important to monitor his blood pressure regularly, and if it drops too low, adjustments may need to be made to the medication regimen.

Overall, the most important thing you can do for your father is to encourage him to follow his treatment plan diligently and maintain a healthy lifestyle. This may include a balanced diet, regular exercise, and avoiding alcohol and other substances that can further damage the liver. It's also important to keep regular appointments with his healthcare provider and report any concerning symptoms or changes in his condition.

A full neurological exam was done and that was normal, and the neurologist did not seem to think that additional imaging (MRI) was needed because my exam was normal.Even though it was a CT scan of the sinuses, they would have seen the brain (particularly frontal lobe) and notified of any abnormalities they could see. answer_icliniq: Hello. You have been elaborate in providing your medical history and that always helps to form an opinion. Instead, I would suggest here to get one electroencephalogram (EEG) done to look for abnormal electrical discharges in the brain during those episodes. EEG done during the time frame when you are frequently having those episodes will be more rewarding in terms of diagnostic importance. With a normal neurological examination and normal MRI, you should stay calm and assured that it is nothing serious. Lastly and importantly, anxiety is the commonest cause of such symptoms. Please start doing mental relaxation exercises, meditation, yoga and observe for improvement. I hope this helps.  Take care.

The fact that you've had a clear CT scan and MRI is reassuring, and does suggest that a structural problem is less likely. However, it's important to consider that certain conditions may not be visible on imaging studies. If your symptoms persist or worsen, or if you develop any other concerning symptoms, it may be worth discussing further evaluation with your healthcare provider. input: Hi doctor,My father is having enlarged prostate and has to undergo prostatectomy surgery. But, it is getting delayed due to ITP (idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura). Right now he is under urine catheter. From past 12 days he is using Silodal-D 8 mg. Doctor said that after 20 days the prostate gland will reduce. Through ultrasound test we came to know the size of prostate enlarged is 54.6 cc. My doubt is, if it is enlarged to 54, is it possible that it can reduce through tablets? Or is it a must to undergo surgery? Right now the platelet count is 40,000. Please suggest a solution for this.
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. Your father was on medication and has history of urine retention in the past. Currently, he is on catheter. Chances of medical management will not be successful. He needs surgery of either TURP (transurethral resection of the prostate) or HoLEP (Holmium laser enucleation of the prostate) for prostate. Size is never a criteria for surgery. Enlarged prostate can shrink by 30% if Dutasteride is given. But, this does not guarantee that the patient will become catheter free. However, it reduces blood vessels in prostate and helps decrease blood loss in surgery. Dutas-T (Dutasteride and Tamsulosin) also contains similar medicines as Silodal D (combination of Dutasteride and Silodosin). His ITP needs to be tackled and with all due precautions and keeping platelet concentrates on standby. He can undergo endoscopic surgery with due risks. Laser surgery - HoLEP will be better for him if available.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello,

Enlarged prostate, also known as benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), can lead to urinary symptoms such as difficulty in urination, increased frequency of urination, and incomplete emptying of the bladder. If BPH is causing significant problems with urination, it may need treatment. Treatment can involve medications or surgery, depending on the severity of the symptoms and the size of the prostate.

Silodal-D 8mg is an alpha-blocker medication that helps to relax the muscles of the prostate gland and bladder neck, which can improve urine flow and other urinary symptoms associated with BPH. It can also help to reduce the size of the prostate gland by shrinking the glandular tissue. However, the size reduction may not be a significant one and is not always guaranteed. It is suitable only for mild to moderate cases of enlargement of the prostate gland.

In severe cases of BPH, where the prostate gland size is large, surgery is recommended. The most common surgery for BPH is transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP), in which the surgeon removes the excess prostate tissue that is blocking the urethra.

Regarding your father's condition, if his urine flow has improved after taking Silodal-D 8mg, then it suggests that the medication is working. However, if he is still having significant urinary symptoms, such as being unable to pass urine despite using the catheter, then he may require further investigations and/or a different treatment plan. Depending on his platelet counts, surgery may have to be delayed. Hence, it is best to follow the advice of the doctor treating your father's condition.

input: Hello doctor,I am a 31 year old female. My first trimester NT and double marker test showed a very low risk of 1:3690 for DS and 1:100000 for two other abnormalities. During the second-trimester anomaly scan, the doctor found two markers, CP cyst and mild RPD 4 mm and 4.5 mm. I have given blood for NIPT but did not get any results. I am at 23 weeks now and stressing out and getting really depressed. I have no family history of abnormalities. Is there a need to worry?

answer_chatgpt:
Hello,

Based on the information you have provided, it seems like your initial screening tests for Down syndrome and other abnormalities had very low risks, which is reassuring. However, the identification of two soft markers during your anomaly scan does increase the risk slightly.

It's good that you have given blood for NIPT (Non-Invasive Prenatal Testing), as this test has a high accuracy rate for detecting Down syndrome and other chromosomal abnormalities. However, it can take some time to receive the results from this test, so it's understandable that you may be feeling anxious and stressed.

In terms of the specific markers identified during your scan, CP cyst and mild RPD, both of these markers are relatively common and are often benign. CP cysts are fluid-filled spaces that can be detected in the brain of the developing fetus. They are usually benign and resolve on their own without causing any problems. Likewise, mild RPD is a common ultrasound finding that usually resolves on its own without causing any problems.

Overall, it's important to remember that soft markers are just that - markers. They do not definitively indicate an abnormality and may resolve on their own. The NIPT test should provide you with more information, but even if it does come back positive for a chromosomal abnormality, it's important to remember that many of these conditions can be managed and that there is support available.

In the meantime, it's important to try and manage your stress levels as best you can. Make sure to seek support from loved ones, and consider speaking with a counselor or therapist if you feel you need additional support.

Best of luck to you.

input: Results from an HSG procedure showed that I have a bicornuate uterus or a thick septum. Both Fallopian tubes are patent. Currently trying to conceive and I\m pretty concerned about the complications with both fertility and pregnancy due to the potential above diagnosis. Further clinical evaluation is recommended. What can be done to fix this and/or make the changes for a healthy full-term pregnancy? Thanks!
answer_icliniq: HiThanks for your querywoman may never need treatment for a bicornuate uterus. If it is required, the surgery is known as a Strassman metroplasty.This surgery may be recommended if a woman has repeated miscarriages and bicornuate uterus is thought to be the cause.Performing this procedure, as a solution to infertility, is controversial, as the majority of research carried out suggests that having a bicornuate uterus will not affect a woman's chances of falling pregnant.Some evidence suggests that problems, such as miscarriage and early birth, are more likely in women with the condition, though it does not rule out a successful pregnancy and natural delivery.Women with a bicornuate uterus who do become pregnant should have additional monitoring and checkups throughout their pregnancy, to minimize risk and identify any problems early on.It's not necessary that all those with this condition will get problems with fertility or pregnancy but the chances are more especially if it's septate uterus where there's full septum diving uterus into two.Sometimes it can't be established on imaging and laparoscopic view may be necessary.  Take care.
answer_chatgpt:
If you have a bicornuate uterus or thick septum, it can affect your fertility and increase the risk of complications during pregnancy such as preterm labor and miscarriage. If there is a cystic nodularity in the right adnexa, further evaluation may be needed to determine the course of action.

In terms of fertility, some women with bicornuate uterus or thick septum can conceive naturally, but others may need fertility treatments such as intrauterine insemination (IUI) or in vitro fertilization (IVF). Your doctor can discuss these options with you and may recommend a referral to a fertility specialist.

During pregnancy, monitoring is important to identify any potential complications early. This may include frequent ultrasounds to monitor fetal growth and cervical length measurements to assess the risk of preterm labor. In some cases, your doctor may recommend a cerclage, which is a stitch that is placed in the cervix to help keep it closed during pregnancy.

Surgical correction of a bicornuate uterus or thick septum may be an option in some cases, but it is not always necessary or recommended. This decision should be made on a case-by-case basis with your doctor.

It is important to work closely with your healthcare provider to develop a plan that is tailored to your individual needs and concerns. With proper care and management, many women with a bicornuate uterus or thick septum can go on to have healthy full-term pregnancies.
answer_chatdoctor: Yes, if your HSG shows a bicornuate uterus then it means that your uterus is not shaped normally. The normal shape for a female is like a tulip with two flaps at the top which are called cornua. You may have one or two cornua missing as well as a thin wall between the two halves of your uterus. This makes conception difficult because the sperm cannot swim up the fallopian tubes to reach the egg. It also means that if you do become pregnant, the baby will not develop properly in the first months of its life. The best way to correct this problem is to have surgery to remove the wall between the two sides of your uterus and reconnect the two sides. This is called a hysterosalpingogram (HSG). You should discuss this operation with your gynecologist.

input: Hello doctor,My father recently had a heart attack and a stroke. Then had two heart valves repaired and they want to put a stent in. His list of medications does not seem to make sense and although he loves his cardiologist. I do not think he is paying enough attention. I am in the medical field and although I am not a pharmacist or an M.D. I know a little bit about adverse drug reactions. Tell me if this makes sense to you.Metoprolol tartrate 25 mg half pill twice a day, Potassium Chloride 20 mEq once daily, Losartan potassium 25 mg once daily, Warfarin sodium 5 mg once daily, Atorvastatin 20 mg, Digozin 25 mcg once daily and Furosemide 40 mg once daily.I need to know if these medications are fine to take all in one day and if so, I would like to split them into two times a day according to the reactions. I feel that so many medications doing the same thing is redundant and unnecessary and causing problems. Please help me.
answer_icliniq: Hi. Let us evaluate all medications your father is receiving. Diagnosis: Valvotomy, CAD with MI (coronary artery disease with myocardial infarction) and CVA (cerebrovascular accident) (recovered). Tablet Metoprolol is essential in patients with heart attacks as it slows the heart and keeps it healthy. So we cannot omit that. The dose is already very low and so we cannot reduce it further. Tablet Losartan, again essential in people with heart attacks. It is a disease-modifying  ChatDoctor.  The dose is already less and cannot be reduced. But yes, the timing can be changed to alternate with Metoprolol (one in the morning and one in the evening). The danger is giving Potassium tablet Losartan and Digoxin and can cause increased potassium levels and giving more potassium is highly dangerous. The doctor might have given for some time but it has to be stopped now. Warfarin is given for stroke prevention. 5 mg is a minimal dose. It cannot be removed as stroke prevention is essential. Timing can be made flexible. Atorvastatin is for heart attack prevention. Ideally can be taken at bedtime. Frusemide is a diuretic. There is no specific reason to give this in your case. The only issue I can think of is because of the valve surgeries they want to keep blood volume low. This can be discussed and removed. Ideally can be given in the morning. About Aspirin, it should not be given when you are giving Warfarin. Remove it if it is still on. If the blood becomes very thin then there are chances of brain hemorrhages. I hope this helps.
answer_chatgpt:
I'm sorry to hear about your father's health issues. The medications your father is taking are commonly used for the treatment of heart conditions. I can understand your concerns regarding the multiple medications and their potential side effects. However, it is important to note that each medication has a specific role in managing your father's condition.

Metoprolol is a beta-blocker that is used to control blood pressure and reduce the workload on the heart.

Potassium chloride is a supplement that helps maintain normal levels of potassium in the body.

Losartan is an angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) that is used to treat high blood pressure and prevent damage to the heart and kidneys.

Warfarin is a blood thinner that is used to prevent blood clots from forming and reduce the risk of stroke in patients with atrial fibrillation or other heart conditions.

Atorvastatin is a cholesterol-lowering medication that helps reduce the risk of heart attack and stroke.

Digozin is a medication that helps regulate heart rhythm and improve heart function.

Furosemide is a diuretic that is used to help reduce excess fluid in the body.

While some of these medications may have overlapping effects, they work in different ways and together can help manage your father's condition. It is important that your father takes each medication as prescribed by his cardiologist.

If you are concerned about potential side effects or interactions with these medications, it may be helpful to schedule an appointment with your father's pharmacist or cardiologist to discuss these concerns. In general, splitting the medications into two doses per day may not be necessary or recommended without consulting with the prescribing doctor first.

I hope this information helps and wish all the best for your father's health.
answer_chatdoctor: Hi, Thanks for writing in. As you have mentioned your fathers history with heart attack and stroke, it seems that he has got coronary artery disease which means blockages in coronary vessels supplying blood to heart muscle. This can lead to chest pain (angina) while exercise or stress. Since your father has been given metoprolol and warfarin, it suggests that he is at risk of clot formation either due to heart attack or atherosclerosis. The other medicines like potassium, losartan, statins etc. are used to control high blood pressure as well as diabetes. These medicines also reduce the chance of further blockage in coronary vessels and hence prevention of future heart attacks. Hence, it is necessary to continue all these medicines. You may discuss with your father's treating doctor regarding splitting the doses of some of the medicines if required.

Depending on the severity of your symptoms, your doctor may recommend one or more of the following treatments:

1. Artificial tears and saliva substitutes: These can help relieve dryness and discomfort.

2. Medications: Your doctor may prescribe medications such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) or immunosuppressants to reduce inflammation and manage symptoms.

3. Punctal plugs: These are small plugs that can be inserted into the tear ducts in your eyes to help retain moisture.

4. Lifestyle changes: Staying hydrated and avoiding triggers such as cigarette smoke and dry environments can help relieve symptoms.

Your doctor will be able to provide you with more specific treatment options once you have been diagnosed with Sjogren's syndrome. I hope this helps!

I performed oral sex on a female. I then fingered her and then masturbated myself with the same hand. I have asked a few professionals and they say I am only at risk theoretically but have no real risk of contracting HIV. However, my body tells me differently. I have had a sore throat for 20 days, no appetite, pain where my liver and spleen are, weakness in my legs that come and go and a hint of nausea every now and then. I have night sweats that awaken me but they are accompanied by panic. My heart feels like it is going to burst. My tongue and mouth burn, but I think that is acid-related because my breath is awful, no matter how much I brush. I had an Alere combo HIV test at 25 days and that was negative. I have never had a fever, rash, swollen nodes, or any of the common symptoms. My questions are what is my real risk of contracting HIV? answer_chatgpt:
Hello,

I'm sorry to hear that you're experiencing such intense anxiety and fear about your sexual encounter. It sounds like you've gotten conflicting information from professionals, which can be confusing and increase anxiety.

Based on the details you've provided, the risk of contracting HIV from performing oral sex on a female is considered low, but not zero. This is because saliva contains enzymes that can break down and inactivate the virus, but there is still a theoretical risk if there was blood or other bodily fluids present. Fingering and masturbating do not typically transmit HIV, unless there was blood or other bodily fluids present and there were open wounds or breaks in the skin.

Your negative HIV test at 25 days is encouraging, but it's important to note that the window period for HIV can vary and some people may take longer to develop detectable antibodies. The health department may have recommended against retesting because the Alere combo test is considered highly sensitive and specific, but a repeat test at a later time period (such as 3 months post-exposure) could provide additional reassurance.

It's possible that your physical symptoms could be related to anxiety and stress, which can manifest in physical ways. However, it's also important to rule out any medical causes for your symptoms, especially if they persist or worsen. I would recommend discussing your symptoms with a healthcare provider and possibly getting a physical exam and other tests as needed.

Lastly, it's important to seek support for your emotional well-being as well. Infidelity and potential exposure to HIV can be very distressing and it's important to have resources to help cope with these feelings. This could include therapy, support groups, or talking with trusted friends or family members.

I hope this information is helpful and reassuring. Please feel free to ask any follow-up questions or concerns.

You have to know that after ovulation in female in their menstrual cycle, progesterone level rises. The level goes down once menstrual the bleeding stops. So the normal range of progesterone varies in different phases of the menstrual cycle. Your wife\s progesterone level seems to be appropriate in the secretory phase means after the ovulation period. So menstrual-related problem is not causative for your infertility problem. I suggest you have sexual intercourse around the ovulation time that is between 12-18 th days of the menstrual cycle to achieve conception. Your semen analysis also should be done for primary workup. answer_chatgpt: A progesterone level of 23.5 ng/ml on day 10 after ovulation is a good result and indicates that ovulation has occurred and an egg has been released. A level above 10 ng/ml typically means that ovulation has occurred. The result also suggests that your wife's luteal phase (the time between ovulation and the start of her next menstrual cycle) is at a healthy length, which is usually between 10-16 days. This is a positive sign for trying to conceive a baby.

Please discuss with your physician about this. Hope this answers your question.

input: Hello doctor,My problem is very common with statistically around 30% of men face, which is premature ejaculation. There is no erectile dysfunction. It is just that I discharge at an extremely swift pace, that is within 30 seconds to 2 minutes of sexual activity. I have tried all techniques like start stop, using a thicker condom, deep breaths, etc., but nothing worked. When I was a child, my prepuce was surgically removed due to some reason, which I assume might have increased the sensitivity. Anxiousness to perform could also be one of the reasons for quick discharge. Kindly suggest me a solution, be it a medicine, pill or lotion that could help me perform better. Please suggest something without hazardous side effects, because this medical glitch is giving invisible dents to my married life. Looking forward to get fruitful advice from your esteemed and erudite panel.
answer_icliniq: Hello. There are many ways to treat premature ejaculation if it is not possibly due to anxiety: So, to summarize, if you tried various techniques and still experience premature ejaculation, then the three medical options are, Performance anxiety and extra sensitivity. Premature ejaculation. Follow up if you have any queries to a sexologist online.- Take care.
answer_chatgpt:
Premature ejaculation can be a frustrating and distressing condition for many men. Fortunately, there are several treatment options available to help you perform better in bed.

One of the most effective treatments for premature ejaculation is a type of antidepressant medication called selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). These drugs are usually prescribed by doctors to treat depression and anxiety, but they can also help delay ejaculation. SSRIs work by delaying the reuptake of serotonin in the brain, thereby increasing the amount of serotonin available to cells. This can help to regulate the process of ejaculation and extend the duration of sexual intercourse.

Another effective treatment option for premature ejaculation is topical anesthetics. These are creams, gels or sprays that contain a mild anesthetic that numbs the penile skin, reducing sensitivity and helping to delay ejaculation. However, it is important to use these products with care, as some men may experience side effects such as reduced sensitivity or decreased sexual pleasure.

It is also important to address any underlying psychological factors that may be contributing to your premature ejaculation. Anxiety, stress or performance-related issues can all contribute to early ejaculation, so working with a therapist or counselor to address these issues can help to improve your sexual performance.

In some cases, a combination of medication and therapy may be needed in order to effectively treat premature ejaculation. Your doctor can help you determine the best course of treatment for your individual needs.

Practice masturbation regularly as it will increase your confidence level and enable you to control yourself in real situation. input: Hi doctor,I am a 49-year-old woman and last month I was hospitalized with a necrotizing soft tissue infection on my thigh. I had two debridement surgeries and was discharged after 5 days. After taking this course, after 15 days the itching was gone but still there was considerable burning and redness and irritation. So, I visited the hospital again and the following course was prescribed: Venusia max cream to be applied morning and afternoon, Dazit tablet to be taken at night for 10 days, Desowen cream to be applied at night for 10 days. Now, this course is also done and though there is a considerable improvement I am still having little redness mostly on one side of the scrotum and also some burning sensation. Kindly help me from this irritation.

It seems to be red scrotum syn ChatDoctor.  It is a chronic disease characterized by redness and burning on the scrotum. If I was your treating doctor, I would recommend you to take capsule Doxycycline 100 mg once daily at bedtime, tablet Gabapentin 300 mg in the morning and tablet Levocetirizine at bedtime. Along with this application of Tacrolimus 0.03 percent ointment on the affected areas at night and moisturizing cream during the daytime. Continue this for three weeks and revert. For more information consult a dermatologist online

It is important to maintain good hygiene in the scrotal area, avoid using harsh soaps or detergents, wear loose-fitting clothing, and avoid excessive sweating in that area. 

You can try using a gentle, fragrance-free moisturizer on the affected area and avoid any potential irritants. Over-the-counter hydrocortisone cream may also provide some relief for the inflammation and itching.

If your symptoms persist, it may be beneficial to see a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment options.

Take care.

input: Hi doctor,Daily after my shower, my face turns red with rash. This has been happening since years. I have changed my soap and face wash several times. Besides, I have lived in many cities; this happens every time and everywhere. What could be the reason? What should I do for it? Please help.
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. From your history it appears that, you are suffering from cholinergic urticaria or heat hives. This condition of being hypersensitive or extremely sensitive to heat or the response of sweating. People with this condition often wonder why they feel itch when they exercise or any time they seem to get hot. It can be sensitivity to cold water also. Heat hives can occur at any age and can come and go often without treatment but in some it persists. As you said, after exposure to hot or cold water the skin will become red and sometimes itchy lesions will appear. Itching or burning sensation during the initial attack is common, and normally goes away when the overall body temperature has cooled. No effective treatment is yet available to cure cholinergic urticaria except avoidance of trigger factors. Tablets like Levocetirizine or Desloratadine are only helpful for short periods. Avoidance of trigger should be your first priority. Oxyhive treatment is sometimes helpful.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello there,

It sounds like you may have a condition called post-inflammatory erythema, which is a type of skin rash that can occur after exposure to certain triggers, such as heat, friction, or abrasive skincare products. A change in climate or humidity can also trigger it. Stress, anxiety, and certain medical conditions or medications can also trigger it.

To help relieve your symptoms, you can try using a gentle cleanser and moisturizer, avoiding hot showers or baths, and using sunscreen to protect your skin from further irritation. You may also want to use a fragrance-free detergent and avoid fabrics that may irritate your skin, such as wool or synthetic materials.

If your symptoms persist or worsen, you should consult a dermatologist who can examine your skin and recommend a treatment plan that is tailored to your specific needs.

I hope this helps!

Regards.

input: Hello doctor,The semen analysis report is in my hand, but I cannot understand it.Liquefaction time- 30 minutes.Duration of abstinence- four days.Appearance- creamy white.Volume 4.0 ml.Ph 8Fructose- Present.Motility- 100.Spermatozoa- rapid 20%, slow 10%, non-progressive motility 5%, immotile 83%.Vitality count- 15 million.Morphology- normal 20%, abnormal 80%.

answer_chatgpt:
Hello,

I am happy to help you interpret your semen analysis report. Let me explain the different components of your report to you:

1. Liquefaction time- This refers to the amount of time it takes for the semen to turn from a gel-like state to a liquid form. Your report indicates that this process took 30 minutes.

2. Duration of abstinence- This indicates the amount of time since your last ejaculation before you provided the sample for testing. In your case, it was four days.

3. Appearance- This describes the general appearance of the semen in terms of its color and consistency. In your case, it was creamy white.

4. Volume- This indicates the quantity of semen that was produced during ejaculation. In your case, it was 4.0 ml.

5. pH- This is a measure of the acidity or alkalinity of the semen. Your report indicates a pH of 8, which is on the alkaline side.

6. Fructose- This indicates the presence of fructose in the semen. Fructose is a sugar that provides energy to the sperm. Your report indicates that it is present.

7. Motility- This refers to the movement of the sperm. In your case, the report indicates that 100% of the sperm are motile. Of these, 20% are moving rapidly, 10% are moving slowly, and 5% are not moving in a progressive manner.

8. Vitality count- This refers to the number of live sperm in the semen sample. Your report indicates that there are 15 million live sperm.

9. Morphology- This refers to the appearance of the sperm in terms of its shape and structure. In your case, the report indicates that 20% of the sperm have a normal morphology, while 80% are abnormal.

I hope this explanation helps you understand your report better. If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to ask.

input: Hi doctor,My wife has completed 39 weeks of pregnancy. We got an ultrasound yesterday, and the report is completely normal. But her labor pain is not getting induced, so the doctor is suggesting cesarean, as the baby\s weight is 3.65 kg. My wife would like to have a normal delivery by inducing pain artificially. Our first baby was normally delivered six years ago, by inducing pain artificially when he was 3.33 kg.My wife is 35 years old, and I am attaching the latest ultrasound reports. Is there any harm to the baby during normal delivery in our case? Is there any chance of the head or shoulder getting stuck? Is the baby\s weight more than normal? What is the possibility of normal delivery after inducing pain? Thanks and regards.
answer_icliniq: Hi. I have seen the reports (attachment removed to protect patient identity), and it is normal. No, delivery is risk-free, normal or caesarean. If her BP (blood pressure) and blood sugar are normal, then there is no contraindication to normal delivery. Regarding the chances of the head getting stuck, it can be assessed only at the onset of labor by internal physical examination of her pelvis and relative size to head of the baby. A baby of this size may be delivered vaginally, but many parameters should be optimum. She had normal delivery six years back, she is 35 now, her pelvic bones and muscles may have become less stretchable, and she may not get good enough contractions, and the labor may become prolonged in that case the baby in the womb may get less oxygen. These are possibilities that may or may not happen, if she goes into labor, she and the baby has to be carefully monitored, and if the things do not go wrong, then she may deliver normally. Until the outcome, no prediction can be made with surety. For more information consult an obstetrician and gynaecologist online
answer_chatgpt:
Hello, 

I understand your concerns. Based on the ultrasound report, your wife's pregnancy appears to be normal. However, if labor has not been initiated spontaneously, the doctor may suggest inducing labor with medications. This is a reasonable option in a low-risk pregnancy. 

The size of the baby (3.65 kg) is larger than average, but it does not necessarily indicate a high-risk pregnancy. Although larger babies are more likely to experience shoulder dystocia (when the baby's shoulders get stuck during delivery), the risk of this occurring in your wife's case is unclear. 

There is a small risk of complications associated with cesarean delivery, such as injury to the uterus, bleeding, and anesthesia risks. Therefore, it is preferable for your wife to deliver vaginally if possible. Only in certain cases, such as fetal distress or failure of induction, is cesarean recommended. 

Ultimately, your doctor will determine the best course of action for your wife and unborn child. I recommend discussing your concerns with your healthcare provider to make an informed decision. 

Best regards.
answer_chatdoctor: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. As your wife is 35 years old, and she had normal delivery for the last child at 3.33 kg now, I think it will be safe to deliver this baby also naturally. In my opinion, there is no problem with induction of labour if you want to do that. The baby should weigh around 4 kilograms. Above 4 kilograms, it may cause some problems in delivery. So, it will be better to avoid induction of labour. Hope this helps. Take care.

Is there any way for me to tell if it is serious or not?
answer_icliniq: Hello. I went through your post. Tingling sensation in any part is due to nerve irritation supplying the area. In case of hand, it can be due to cervical spondylosis or due to CTS (carpal tunnel syn ChatDoctor.  However, none is serious and can be managed easily. So, no need to worry. You should check your vitamin D level and B12 level. These deficiencies can also cause tingling.  Take care.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello! Tingly sensation or tingling in the hand can be caused by several reasons. The most common cause is nerve compression or pinching, which can occur due to poor posture, prolonged sitting in the same position, or sleeping on the arm in a strange position. Another cause can be carpal tunnel syndrome, which affects the median nerve in the hand and wrist. Other possible causes include diabetes, hypothyroidism, vitamin deficiency, or anxiety.

It is important to see a doctor to assess the cause of your tingling sensation. A doctor will take a detailed medical history, perform a physical exam, and order additional tests as necessary. Depending on the cause, treatment options could be as simple as changing your posture or sleeping position, taking vitamin supplements, or wearing a brace or splint. In other cases, medications or surgery may be needed.

If the tingling sensation persists or is associated with other symptoms such as weakness, pain, or swelling, it is important to seek medical attention immediately.

input: Hello doctor,I have high eye pressure of 30 and 24, visual field loss, and at times I go completely blind in both eyes. I have also gone blind in just one eye. I just went back to the eye doctor when I had the pressure of 24 and 30. I have been there three times and each time I had a high eye pressure. He at first told me everything looked good until I asked him what my pressure was again because he specifically said my pressure was good when I knew that it was high. He told me that it was fairly high when I asked for it but he says I do not have any optic nerve damage and that it is highly unlikely that I have glaucoma because I am 23 years old. But my research has told me differently that high eye pressure alone does not mean that that you have glaucoma but if you have visual field loss or optic nerve damage with your high eye pressure then you do. So I do not know what is going on? Can you please help me understand and is it possible that I do have glaucoma and should go to another doctor?
answer_icliniq: Hello, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. I reviewed your history. The presence of elevated intraocular pressure is called as ocular hypertension. If it is associated with visual field changes or optic disc changes it is called as glaucoma. However, ocular hypertension alone also requires treatment particularly in your case as intraocular pressure is high. Hence you need to consult glaucoma specialist for evaluation and advice accordingly. Increased intraocular pressure. Visual fields, OCT (optic coherence tomography). Glaucoma. Antiglaucoma  ChatDoctor.  Once in six months.
answer_chatgpt:
Based on what you have described, it is definitely possible that you have glaucoma or some other condition that is causing high eye pressure and visual field loss. While it is true that glaucoma is more common in older individuals, it can still occur in younger people, especially if there is a family history of the disease. 

It is important to note that high eye pressure is just one of many risk factors for glaucoma. Other factors include family history, ethnicity, age, and previous eye injuries. So, even if your doctor is not seeing any damage to your optic nerve at this point, it is still important to monitor your eye pressure regularly and to continue to get regular eye exams.

I would recommend getting a second opinion from another eye doctor, preferably one who specializes in glaucoma. They may want to perform additional tests, such as a visual field test or an optic nerve scan, to get a better idea of what is going on with your eyes. It is always better to err on the side of caution when it comes to your vision health.

input: Hi doctor,I am just wondering if retainers can be used to realign teeth? I have had braces off for a month and have worn mine religiously but it feels like my teeth are relapsing a little bit despite this. Is this possible? Namely, my teeth before braces were not aligned properly, had spaces, and my ortho had trouble aligning them, as they are straight but going across diagonally, but we worked on this and got it better.When my braces came off, they felt straight, I was really happy. Now, it seems like my teeth are going diagonally and not aligned anymore and is causing anxiety. I want my teeth to come straight down and not be slanted. Also, can my two front teeth be improved by filing? I have already visited my ortho once to have my essix fixed, and my Hawley\s was severely tightened during that time, so I feel like contacting them just a couple of weeks later again.
answer_icliniq: Hello, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. Once the braces are removed, you have to wear the retainers for at least eight months. You have to wear them literally all the time. Else there are high chances for relapse. And yes your front teeth can be filled using tooth colored composite restoration. You just have to visit the dentist every 30- 45 days to lightly activate the retainer. Please be careful regarding this. If the teeth relapse beyond an extent, then you probably will have to restart the treatment all over again. I hope this helps.
answer_chatgpt:
Yes, retainers can be used to realign teeth, but it depends on the severity of the relapse. It is possible for teeth to shift slightly even when wearing a retainer, especially if the retainer is not fitting properly or if it is not being worn as directed. 

It is also possible that your teeth were not fully aligned when the braces were removed and have continued to shift slightly over time. In either case, it is important to contact your orthodontist and schedule a follow-up appointment to evaluate your teeth and determine the best course of action to correct any shifts. 

Regarding improving your front teeth through filing, it would depend on the specific issues with your teeth. Filing may be a possible solution for minor reshaping or to correct slight irregularities, but it may not be suitable for more significant alignment issues or gaps between teeth. Your orthodontist can evaluate your teeth and provide recommendations for the best treatment options.
answer_chatdoctor: Hi, Thanks for writing in. Retainers cannot help improve the alignment of teeth, however, they can prevent any relapse of the teeth back into their previous position. The use of retainers should be used after orthodontic treatment to keep the teeth in its new position. If your teeth are moving towards its old position then there must be some reason why you need to see an Orthodontist immediately. The filing of two front teeth can be done only when the orthodontist has finished with the alignment of the teeth. Please do not worry.

Hello! Sorry to hear that the medications you have tried so far haven't worked for your acne. There are several options and approaches that we can explore to help manage and improve your condition. 

Firstly, it's important to determine the type and severity of your acne so that a personalized treatment plan can be developed. Mild acne can be treated with over-the-counter topical medications that contain benzoyl peroxide, salicylic acid, or alpha-hydroxy acids. These may help to reduce inflammation and prevent clogging of pores. 

If the acne is moderate to severe, prescription medications may be necessary. These can include topical retinoids, which help to unclog pores and reduce inflammation, or oral antibiotics, which can help to control bacterial growth in the skin. 

In some cases, hormonal imbalances may also contribute to acne. Hormonal therapy, such as birth control pills, can be effective in managing acne in females. 

Other treatments that can be beneficial include chemical peels, microdermabrasion, and laser therapy. These procedures help to exfoliate the skin, reduce inflammation, and stimulate collagen production, resulting in smoother, clearer skin.

In order to determine the best treatment plan for you, I recommend scheduling a consultation with a dermatologist who can further evaluate your skin and recommend appropriate treatment options.

Dear Patient,

Betnelan is a steroid medication, also known as Betamethasone, commonly used in the treatment of asthma. As with any medication, there are potential side effects that you should be aware of:

1. Increased risk of infection: Steroids can suppress the immune system, making it easier for infections to occur.

2. Weight gain: Betnelan can cause fluid retention and increased appetite, which can lead to weight gain.

3. High blood pressure: Long-term use of Betnelan can increase blood pressure.

4. Diabetes: Steroids can raise blood sugar levels, which can lead to the development of diabetes.

5. Mood changes: Betnelan can cause mood swings, depression, or anxiety.

6. Cataracts and glaucoma: Prolonged use of steroids may increase the risk of cataracts and glaucoma in some individuals.

7. Osteoporosis: Long-term use of Betnelan can weaken bones and increase the risk of fractures.

It is important to note that these side effects are more common with long-term use or with high doses of Betnelan. If you have concerns about your medication, it is important to discuss them with your doctor. Your doctor may recommend different treatment options or adjust your dosage to minimize the risk of side effects.
answer_chatdoctor: Dear user, Betelan contains albuterol which is a short-acting beta agonist (SAGA) used in the treatment of bronchospasm associated with asthma and other respiratory disorders. It produces its action by decreasing the resistance of the small airways to flow of air out of the lungs. Albuterol has been reported to cause systemic adverse reactions, such as palpitations, tremor, headache, nervousness, dizziness, and sleeplessness. These symptoms usually disappear after stopping or reducing the dose of albuterol. Other less common but serious adverse reactions include psychiatric disturbances (such as hallucinations), cardiac arrhythmia, severe hypokalemia, and angioedema. The most common side effect of albuterol is transient increase in heart rate. Less common side effects are

input: Hello doctor,CPAM type 3 lesions, but ratio is 0.6. Will it cause a serious problem after birth for my baby?
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. I have gone through your history and understand your concern. As there is no chromosomal abnormalities in your baby, there is most likely no problem in future but continuous monitoring is important in your case.
answer_chatgpt:
CPAM (Congenital Pulmonary Airway Malformation) type 3 lesions represent a rare genetic condition affecting the lungs during fetal development. The condition occurs due to abnormal lung tissue formation leading to the formation of cysts in the lung tissues.

The ratio you mentioned could be the observed-to-expected lung-to-head ratio (O/E LHR), which is used to predict the severity of CPAM. An O/E LHR less than 1 indicates partial volume of lung compression, and values below this threshold may pose a potentially greater risk.

However, even with a ratio of 0.6, it is difficult to predict how severe the condition could be until the baby is born. In some cases, babies with CPAM type 3 may have mild symptoms and may not require any treatment. In other cases, however, the condition may be severe and require immediate medical intervention.

It is important to monitor the pregnancy carefully and have regular follow-ups with your doctor to ensure the baby's health is closely monitored. If the baby shows any signs of respiratory distress after the birth, the healthcare provider may choose to observe the baby, provide supportive care, or may recommend surgery.

Overall, CPAM type 3 can be unpredictable, but with proper medical care and management, the risks to the baby can be minimized.

When you have post nasal drip, the excess mucus can travel down the throat and cause inflammation and irritation in the Eustachian tubes. This can lead to a feeling of blockage in your ears, and also cause ringing or buzzing sounds (tinnitus).

If your ear testing results were normal, then it is likely that your symptoms are due to the post nasal drip. Antibiotics may not be effective in treating post nasal drip, but there are other treatments available that can help to relieve your symptoms. These may include:

1. Nasal decongestants: Decongestant sprays or nasal drops can help to reduce congestion and inflammation in the nasal passages, which can help to relieve post nasal drip.

2. Saline nasal rinses: Using a saline solution to rinse your nasal passages can help to clear out excess mucus and reduce inflammation.

3. Antihistamines: If your post nasal drip is caused by allergies, then antihistamines may be helpful in reducing your symptoms.

4. Steroid nasal sprays: These sprays can help to reduce inflammation in the nasal passages, which can decrease post nasal drip.

5. Reflux medication: If your post nasal drip is caused by acid reflux, then medications that reduce stomach acid can be useful in relieving your symptoms.

I'm sorry to hear that you are experiencing tinnitus. While there is no cure for tinnitus, there are various management techniques that can help ease the symptoms. Here are some suggestions:

1. Sound therapy: This involves using external sounds to mask or distract from the tinnitus. This can include white noise machines, music, or nature sounds.

2. Cognitive-behavioral therapy: This type of therapy can help you learn to cope with the emotional and psychological effects of tinnitus.

3. Acoustic neuromodulation: This is a newer technique that uses medical devices to deliver acoustic stimulation to the auditory nervous system. This can help rewire the brain's response to tinnitus.

4. Ginkgo biloba: Some studies have suggested that ginkgo biloba may improve blood flow to the brain and alleviate tinnitus symptoms, though more research is needed to confirm this.

5. Medications: Certain medications, such as tricyclic antidepressants and anticonvulsants, may help relieve tinnitus symptoms.
