answer_icliniq: Hi. Otosclerosis is a disease in which the tiny bone of the middle ear gets fixed resulting in hearing loss. Unfortunately, there is no medicine to cure otosclerosis. answer_chatgpt:

Factors such as your overall health, the severity of your hearing loss, and your personal preferences should also be considered. Additionally, there may be other treatment options available, such as hearing aids or cochlear implants, that can help improve your hearing.

It is also important to note that while otosclerosis can run in families, it is not always hereditary. Regular hearing screenings can help monitor changes in your hearing and detect any problems early on.

Your wife's ferritin level is lower than normal, which is unusual for individuals with Hemoglobin E trait, as it is usually elevated. However, there can be several reasons for low ferritin levels, such as poor diet, chronic blood loss, or inflammation.

The doctor may want to investigate further to determine the cause of your wife's low ferritin levels. It is also essential to regularly monitor her hemoglobin levels to ensure that they remain stable, especially during pregnancy or if she plans to have more children.

In terms of passing on Hemoglobin E trait to your children, the condition is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, meaning both parents must pass on an abnormal gene for their child to have the condition. If one parent has Hemoglobin E trait, there is a 50% chance of passing it on to their child. However, the child will not typically experience any symptoms unless they inherit two copies of the abnormal gene, one from each parent.

It is essential to discuss your concerns with your doctor and consider genetic counseling if you plan to have more children. They can provide you with more information on the risks and how to manage the condition.

input: Hi doctor,I (34) and my wife (30) have been trying to conceive for about 15 months. There was a small number of leukocytes found too. The doctor wrote a prescription for antibiotics which he said should clear it out. Our concern now is, could the E-coli be the reason for infertility? My wife has not taken antibiotics for a long time and we are presuming that she could have had the bacteria there for a long time. Antibiotics are usually recommended to treat bacterial vaginosis, as it can increase the chances of success with IVF. However, the presence of E. coli alone is not typically associated with infertility.

It is possible that the bacterial vaginosis caused by E. coli could be contributing to your fertility struggles. However, it is important to keep in mind that infertility is often multifactorial, and there may be other factors at play as well.

My advice would be to complete the course of antibiotics prescribed by your doctor and then follow up with a fertility specialist to discuss next steps. They may want to do further testing, such as a semen analysis for you or an HSG (hysterosalpingogram) for your wife, to check for any other potential issues contributing to infertility.

I hope this helps and wish you the best of luck on your fertility journey.

answer_chatdoctor: 110 mg / dL (6.1 mmol / L) fasting blood glucose level is normal. That means your body is able to produce sufficient insulin to maintain your blood glucose at this level after not eating for about 10 hours. You should check your blood glucose again between 45 minutes and 90 minutes after eating something containing carbohydrates. It will be higher than the fasting level. This rise in blood glucose is due to the action of insulin which your body produces to digest the food. After that it should come down again to the fasting level within two hours. This is called the peak response or the area under curve (AUC). The AUC value can help to know how well your body is responding to the insulin produced by the pancreas. The AUC values of more than 80% are considered good.

If ovulation is occurring, then there is a chance of pregnancy. But, most of the time there is either no ovulation or there is bad quality ovulation happening in an irregular cycle. Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) causes hormonal imbalances in a woman's body, leading to problems with ovulation and fertility. It is possible for women with PCOS to get pregnant, but it may take longer than average. Managing PCOS symptoms and improving fertility requires medication and lifestyle changes.

Regarding the E7 measurement on the eighth day of the menstrual period, it is likely a reference to the thickness of the endometrium, or the lining of the uterus. A thickness of 9.3 mm indicates a normal endometrium.

As for the medication, Mychiro is a nutritional supplement that contains myo-inositol, which is believed to improve ovulation and insulin resistance in women with PCOS. It is a safe and commonly prescribed treatment for PCOS-related infertility.

It is important to have patience during the fertility treatment process. Typically, you won't be diagnosed as having infertility unless you have been trying to conceive for at least a year. However, it is recommended to see a fertility specialist if you have any concerns about your fertility.

I hope this clarifies your concerns.

I have read somewhere that they are called floaters and can cause blindness. I have started to see just one dot and now I see some more not too many but irritating. It has been years. However, I have only tried to tell a doctor but he said that there is no problem and I have not done any treatment. So, I need your help, please.
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. You have correctly described your floaters. Only thing is that it is perfectly normal as they are part of aging and will only increase with time. They develop as the vitreous jelly inside our eyes liquifies with time. So they move when your eye moves. They do not cause blindness on their own. They are a matter of concern only when there is a sudden shower of floaters associated with flashes. In that case, you will need an examination of your retina after full dilation of your pupil to rule out any break in your retina which if not lasered on time may proceed to retinal detachment. Take care.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello. Thank you for reaching out to me about your concerns. Based on your description, it sounds like you may be experiencing floaters. Floaters are usually caused by small pieces of debris that float in the vitreous humor inside the eye. They cast a shadow on the retina and can appear as dots, lines or cobwebs that move around in your visual field.

Floaters are generally not a serious problem and do not usually cause blindness. However, if you suddenly see a large number of new floaters, or if you see flashes of light, this could be a sign of a more serious condition called a retinal detachment.

If the floaters are causing significant discomfort or interfering with your vision, you may want to consult an eye specialist (ophthalmologist) for a more thorough eye examination. Your doctor can check to see if there is any underlying eye condition and to see if any treatment is required.

In the meantime, try to avoid looking at bright lights or surfaces and try to rest your eyes when you can. Also, make sure to get plenty of rest, eat a balanced diet, and avoid smoking.

I hope this information has been helpful to you. Please do not hesitate to seek medical advice if you continue to experience discomfort or changes in your vision.

One is an inflammation of the conjunctiva (conjunctivitis) which causes redness and watering of the eye. The other possibility is of a condition known as cataract. Cataract occurs due to aging changes in the lens of the eye. This leads to cloudiness of the lenses, and this prevents clear vision. You should consult an ophthalmologist who will examine your eyes and decide whether you require treatment. There is no need to worry about blindness. Take care.

input: Hello doctor,My dad recently had an echo done which showed valve issues. The cardiologist wants to do a Lexiscan. I thought there are less invasive tests like calcium scoring that would give the same information. Why would the Lexiscan be recommended over other tests?
answer_icliniq: Hello. Calcium score is not as informative as Lexiscan. It is a screening test and not a diagnostic. CT coronary angiography may yield a better information, but has false positive and negative rates. However, CT coronary angiography may be used as an alternative rather than calcium score. Also, treadmill test is an alternative to Lexiscan if he is able to perform exercises. Anyway, Lexiscan is much sensitive than calcium score for detection of blockages. Hope this helps you and get back if you have any doubts. For more information consult a cardiologist online
answer_chatgpt:
Hello, 

Lexiscan is a type of nuclear stress test that is performed to evaluate the blood flow to the heart muscle and identify any potential blockages in the coronary arteries. It involves the injection of a small amount of radioactive tracer into the bloodstream followed by a medication that increases the blood flow to the heart, allowing the tracer to be detected by a special camera.

Calcium scoring, on the other hand, is a CT scan that measures the amount of calcium buildup in the coronary arteries. It is a non-invasive test, but it is not as reliable in identifying blockages in the artery.

In your dad's case, the cardiologist may have recommended the Lexiscan over the calcium scoring because it is a more accurate test in identifying any narrowing or blockages in the coronary arteries. It can also help to determine the severity of the valve issues and whether any further interventions or treatments are necessary.

It is best to discuss the pros and cons of both tests with your dad's cardiologist and ask any questions you may have before making a decision. They can provide more specific information about your dad's condition and which test is most appropriate for him.

Genital warts are caused by the human papilloma virus (HPV), which is highly contagious and can be spread through sexual contact. Even though you have always used a condom during sexual intercourse, there is still a risk of transmission of HPV, as the virus can be present on areas not covered by the condom, such as the scrotum, vulva, and anus.

It is also possible for the virus to remain in the body for a long time after initial infection without causing any symptoms. So it is possible that you may have contracted the virus some time ago without realizing it.

In terms of your treatment, it is important to continue taking the medication as prescribed by your doctor. Dicaris is often used to stimulate the body's immune system to fight the HPV infection. Additionally, the use of Zincolak and Zycolchin may help to boost your immune system, which can also aid in fighting the virus.

It is important to note that there is currently no cure for HPV, so treatment is aimed at managing the symptoms and reducing the risk of transmission to others. Even after warts are removed, there is always a chance of recurrence.

To reduce your risk of future infections and to prevent transmission to others, it is important to practice safe sex by using a condom during sexual intercourse and limiting your number of sexual partners. You may also want to discuss the HPV vaccine with your doctor, as it can help to protect against certain strains of the virus.

Finally, it's important to follow up with your doctor regularly to monitor your condition and ensure that treatment is working effectively.

input: Hi doctor,My girlfriend is 35 years old. She is obese, exercises regularly and tries to watch her diet. In spite of these, she is prediabetic. Her fasting blood glucose is typically around 105 mg/dL. Her A1C is normally in the range of 5.7% to 6%. She takes Metformin to manage the prediabetes. Her blood pressure is checked regularly and is always good. Last time, her blood pressure was 120/80 mmHg. Her eGFR has been tested several times and is always above 90.Recently, she had a medical checkup in her office, and the report was obesity and protein in the urine. So, she went to She went to her GP who performed a 24-hour protein in the urine test. 520 mg per 24 hours was the result of this test. She was referred to an endocrinologist and a nephrologist. The nephrologist did perform an ultrasound. Neither the endocrinologist or the nephrologist were terribly concerned. My girlfriend discussed using ACE inhibitors with the nephrologist, but he did not think they were necessary as her blood pressure was normal. He recommended diet, exercise, weight loss and monitoring.The nephrologist did not tell her that she has phase 1 CKD. But, it sounds like she has. Is this correct? Both the endocrinologist and the nephrologist felt that it would not impact her ability to live a healthy life. Is this correct? If she has CKD, how long will it take to progress? What is the long-term prognosis? Is healthy pregnancy possible? Apart from diet, exercise and weight loss, are there other things you would like to recommend?
answer_icliniq: Hi. Welcome to ChatDoctor. We are here to help you. Considering her prediabetes a cause of her proteinuria, yes, she would be categorized in stage 1 CKD (chronic kidney disease). However, that is very unlikely that such a prediabetes at this age is going to cause proteinuria. Yes, this is not going to impact if does not worsen further. It will not progress much as her diabetes is well controlled. The prognosis of her condition is good, and a healthy pregnancy without any complication is possible. I want to mention that she needs a repeat 24 hours protein test done after a month to reach a conclusion as a single time urine protein can occur due to many causes like heavy exercises and fever. I would recommend ACE inhibitors, ANA (anti-nuclear antibody), C3, C4 level test and ultrasound kidneys in case her urine protein persists even after one month.
answer_chatgpt:
Based on the information provided, it appears that your girlfriend is in the early stages of chronic kidney disease (CKD) with a urine protein excretion of 520 mg per 24 hours. The diagnosis of CKD is made when there is evidence of kidney damage (in this case protein in the urine) for more than three months, regardless of the cause. 

It is good that she is under the care of both an endocrinologist and a nephrologist who have recommended lifestyle modifications, including diet, exercise, weight loss, and monitoring. These are important interventions that can slow the progression of CKD and reduce the risk of other complications. ACE inhibitors and other medications may be considered in the future if her kidney function declines, but for now, the focus should be on addressing the underlying causes and maintaining her overall health.

The rate of progression of CKD can vary depending on the underlying cause, the level of kidney function, and other factors. It is important for her to have regular follow-up with her healthcare providers to monitor her kidney function and adjust her treatment plan as needed.

The long-term prognosis for CKD is variable and depends on many factors, including the underlying cause, the degree of kidney damage, and the effectiveness of treatment. With proper management, many people with CKD can maintain stable kidney function for many years and live a healthy life.

Pregnancy can be challenging for women with CKD, and it is recommended that she discuss this with her healthcare provider if she plans to become pregnant. With proper management and close monitoring, healthy pregnancy is possible, but it is important to understand the risks and potential complications.

In addition to diet, exercise, and weight loss, other strategies that may be helpful for people with CKD include managing blood pressure and cholesterol levels, avoiding tobacco and excessive alcohol consumption, and managing other health conditions such as diabetes and heart disease.

Overall, it is important for her to work closely with her healthcare team to manage her prediabetes and kidney disease, and to take steps to maintain her overall health and well-being.
answer_chatdoctor: 1. CKD is usually caused by diabetes, hypertension, family history of kidney disease etc. It can be either acute or chronic. Chronic CKD needs treatment to slow down the progression of disease. Since you didn't mention any details about your friends condition I am unable to comment more.2. Pregnancy is possible if the kidneys function well.3. Diet and exercise are very important for everyone especially for someone with CKD.4. There are many medications available which can help control the disease. Please consult your doctors before taking any medicine. Hope this helps.

input: Hello doctor,I am a patient of PCOD. I had my last periods before nine days. I took tablet Letrozole 2.5 mg from the second day of my periods and continued for five days. I will be able to meet my husband only after three days.My query is when shall I take injection hCG, as I can afford to have intercourse only after three days. answer_icliniq: Hello, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. As the follicular size has already crossed 22 mm, there are high chances that you would ovulate in the next 24 hours naturally. However, by rule hCG dose is offered when the largest follicular size has crossed 20 mm, so you should be taking it today. After the injection, the ovulation is expected in the next 36 hours after the LH (luteinizing hormone) surge. So we advise intercourse from the very next day of hCG injection daily for the next three days to increase chances of conception. After ovulation, the egg is available for the next 24 hours when fertilization can occur as the egg starts to degenerate post 24 hours of ovulation. It has to be followed daily transvaginal ultrasound to watch for ovulation. If you ovulate by today night and see a ruptured follicle tomorrow then next 24 hours would be crucial for conception. In your case where sex is planned on three days from now if the follicle does not rupture naturally then you can take an hCG injection in the morning before the day of meeting your husband. And you expect egg after ovulation to be viable till that day and the next day when you can opt for frequent sex to increase chances of conception. All this provided follicle does not rupture until tomorrow evening.

Hello,

For your first query, if you want to time the ovulation after hCG injection, you can take the injection when the follicle reaches a size of 18-20mm. In your case, since the follicle has already reached the size of 23.4mm, you can take the injection immediately as it takes about 36-40 hours for ovulation to occur after hCG injection. This will ensure that the ovulation is timed appropriately for optimal chances of conception.

Regarding your second query, PCOD patients are prone to developing cysts, especially if they are on ovulation induction medications. However, regular monitoring with ultrasound can help detect any cyst formation at an early stage, and appropriate management can be initiated if required.

Hope this helps!
answer_chatdoctor: 1) The best time to take HCG is when your follicles reach about 20-21 mm. This is usually around 36 hours before ovulation. It is more likely in cases where the ovaries are enlarged or polycystic. So try to get pregnant by natural means first. If that fails, then you can go ahead with HCG.

input: Hello doctor,I have a braces-related question. I wore the traditional metal braces on my upper jaw as a teen, 10 years ago. My front four teeth shifted a lot (they are crowded, so it was a quite visible shift, some were pushed out, and some pushed back).The retainer could make a big difference in one night when I wore it, but I was advised to get a completely new brace. So a few days ago, I got a lingual brace on the front teeth (the social six). My concern now is the speed of shifting. A couple of hours after getting the braces, my teeth were significantly straighter (1 to 2 mm difference).A day after they look almost perfect, I was told I would be wearing the braces for six months to a year. How is such a drastic change possible so quickly, and is it healthy or normal? It hurts always, but it is not unbearable. And could I have used a new retainer instead of getting new braces then? If you have been told six months, does not mean six months always, if there will be good results, it might be reduced to four to five months even. So go ahead confidently, your dentist is doing right.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello! I can understand your concerns about the speed of shifting with your new lingual braces. It is normal for some initial movement to occur quickly after getting braces, especially with lingual braces, which are placed on the inside of the teeth and provide more force than traditional braces.

However, it is important to note that the speed of movement should be controlled and gradual to avoid damaging the teeth, roots, or surrounding tissues. Your orthodontist has likely developed a treatment plan that will ensure that the speed of movement is appropriate for your specific case.

As for using a new retainer instead of getting new braces, it is possible that a retainer could help maintain your teeth in their current position, but it may not be able to correct the crowding and spacing issues that you are experiencing. Your orthodontist will have assessed your teeth and determined that braces are the best option for achieving the desired results.

It is normal for braces to cause discomfort at first, but the level of pain should not be unbearable. If you are experiencing significant pain, it is important to speak with your orthodontist as they may be able to adjust the braces to make them more comfortable.

Overall, I would recommend that you continue to follow your orthodontist's treatment plan and attend all scheduled appointments to ensure that your teeth are moving safely and effectively. If you have any additional concerns, be sure to bring them up with your orthodontist at your next appointment.
answer_chatdoctor: Hi, Thanks for writing to Chat Doctor. Braces work by applying pressure on the teeth. This pressure causes movement of the teeth in the direction of the force applied. input: Hello doctor,I just recently had an echocardiogram. The result was normal. The only finding was trivial regurgitation of my mitral valve. I was told this is normal. The question I have is, is this something that could get worse over time? If so, what lifestyle changes can I make now to prevent that from happening? I am 42 years old.
answer_icliniq: Hello. Trivial mitral regurgitation is physiological, which means normal. And in absence of structural heart abnormalities, this will not progress. So, there is nothing to worry about it and needs no specific precautions. You may continue with all your daily activities including physical exercises.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello! Trivial Mitral regurgitation (MR) is a common condition that is usually harmless and does not require any treatment. MR occurs when the mitral valve allows a small amount of blood to leak back into the left atrium during the contraction of the heart. In most people with trivial MR, the condition does not progress over time and does not cause any problems.

However, there are some lifestyle changes that may help to prevent the progression of MR. These lifestyle changes include maintaining a healthy weight, exercising regularly, eating a balanced diet, reducing stress, avoiding smoking, limiting alcohol intake, and managing any underlying medical conditions such as high blood pressure or diabetes. Additionally, it is important to attend regular follow-up appointments with your doctor to monitor your heart health and MR.

If you do experience progression of your MR, it may eventually require treatment, such as medication or surgery, depending on the severity of the condition. However, it is important to note that this is rare with trivial MR. Overall, living a healthy lifestyle and having regular follow-up appointments with your doctor is the best way to prevent the progression of MR.
answer_chatdoctor: 1) Mild Mitral Valve Regurgitation (MR) is very common in people your age and usually does not require any treatment unless it becomes symptomatic or progresses to moderate/severe MR. 2) Exercise regularly, eat healthy foods, avoid fatty foods, quit smoking if you do smoke, maintain ideal body weight, etc. will help reduce the risk of further progression of MR.

input: Hello doctor,I am a 65-year-old white male and tested negative for HIV 1 and 2 in a one-time test three to four years ago. I am not sure if it was a third or fourth generation test. I tested negative with a fourth generation test four months back because I thought I might be having some symptoms. And I remembered the flu-like illness about six months after exposure.I weighed 204 kg three months back and now I weigh 191 kg. I have worried a lot (pacing the floor, sleep loss) for past five months, took Paxil, and realized my worry could account for some weight loss. Shortly, after the test, I had the following symptoms:I started having a small patch of 8 to 10 pinpoint sized pink-light red dots under the skin of my left wrist that have been there for two to three weeks.I also had a smooth, flat spot about 3/8 inch in diameter that appeared on the back of my right hand about a week ago, that has now faded to a pinkish brown. I have read about Kaposi sarcoma being a definer of AIDS, but do not understand how that could happen with a non-reactive fourth generation test four months back.Three months back, I had sinus type headaches multiple times that would sometimes be severe. After three rounds of antibiotics and a CT scan two months back that showed shadowing in a sinus, my ENT doctor just told me this week that there is something in my sphenoid sinus. He is treating it with a rinse for inflammation for three weeks before I see him again. I have had blood in my mucous from my nose for past three months. It dries out in my nose and causes blood almost every time I pick at it. The ENT doctor said that the lining of my nose had been worn down from this, but did not seem concerned about it. I do mess with it a lot, and blow my nose a lot.My PSA elevated quickly in the last six months, going from 1.2 to 3.5. A biopsy two months back showed an early cancer in one of the 12 regions biopsied.I had a lithotripsy on an 8 mm kidney stone this month and I am still passing dark urine. I was told that I do have several small stones still in each kidney. I have had multiple stones over the years.I have a swelling sensation all the way around my waist down into my groin at times that has been there for about two months, but no noticeable swelling. Several doctors have felt in my abdomen and detected nothing abnormal. A CT scan with no contrast 10 days ago showed no abnormalities other than a little swelling in the right kidney where the 8 mm stone was blocking the passage into the ureter. A CT with contrast five months back was normal except for showing stones. But I have microscopic blood in my urine since then.My vital signs have consistently been normal for past five months, including temperature, BP and oxygen. I know I am getting old and some of these symptoms happen with age. But it is driving me crazy that so much has happened since my negative fourth generation test which is supposed to detect antibodies may not be conclusive after three to four years of exposure. I had Zoloft 100 mg daily earlier but currently, on Paxil 40 mg and Propranolol 60 mg daily.After nearly four years of exposure and a negative antibody test four months back, would that test have detected antibodies if I were in a stage of HIV or AIDS? Do you think it was conclusive? Or is there another test I should have done? I have read about an HIV RNA test, but it says early detection. I understand that it detects more than the antibodies. What are your thoughts on my next step?
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. Your test done before four months is certainly conclusive and final. A three months intervening period/window period between risk exposure and a fourth generation HIV screening test is good enough for it to be considered conclusive. 95 % of those infected with HIV will have detectable antibodies by four weeks and by 12 weeks, or three months 100 % of HIV infected individual will have detectable antibodies. Antibodies once formed will remain detectable by screening tests. In fact, antibody based HIV screening tests are the gold standard tests for the diagnosis of infection whereas an HIV RNA PCR test is more commonly utilized as a measure of response to antiretroviral therapy in HIV AIDS patients rather than as a diagnostic or screening test. For more information consult a HIV AIDS specialist online
answer_chatgpt:
As per your history, you have tested negative for HIV 1 and 2 using fourth-generation tests, which are the most accurate and reliable for detecting infection. A negative test result four months ago means that you do not currently have HIV infection, regardless of any symptoms you may be experiencing. It is also unlikely that you could have developed AIDS after three to four years of exposure without being diagnosed with HIV infection, as AIDS is a late stage of HIV disease.

The symptoms you are experiencing can be due to various other conditions, and it is important for you to continue working with your healthcare providers to determine the cause of each symptom and manage them accordingly. Some of your symptoms, such as sinus headaches, urinary stones, and early prostate cancer, are common conditions that are typically not related to HIV infection. 

With regard to your concerns about HIV testing, the fourth-generation tests are very accurate and reliable, and the likelihood of false-negative results is very low. It is not necessary to repeat an HIV test if you have a negative result on a fourth-generation test done four months ago, but if you are still concerned, you can consider an HIV RNA test which can detect the virus itself within a few days to weeks after infection. However, it is important to remember that negative results on the RNA test would not change the interpretation of your previous negative HIV tests.

In conclusion, you can rest assured that your negative HIV test results four months ago are conclusive and reliable, and it is unlikely that your symptoms are related to HIV infection. It is important to continue working with your healthcare providers to manage your symptoms and to address any concerns you may have.

1. Rest: Reduce the amount of time you spend on your feet and avoid activities that put pressure on your ankles.

2. Ice: Apply ice to your ankle for 15-20 minutes several times a day to help reduce swelling.

3. Compression: Wear compression stockings or bandages to help support your ankle and reduce swelling.

4. Elevation: Elevate your ankle above your heart whenever possible to help reduce swelling.

If your symptoms persist, it's important to see a doctor for a proper diagnosis and treatment. A doctor may recommend physical therapy, medication, or other treatments depending on the cause of your ankle swelling and pain.

In the meantime, be sure to warm up and stretch properly before jogging and wear supportive shoes to help prevent further injury.

However, both SMILE and Contoura Vision are advanced and effective procedures for correcting vision, and the choice between the two should be based on the evaluation and recommendation of your eye surgeon after conducting various diagnostic tests.

SMILE is a flapless, minimally invasive procedure that involves creating a small incision in the cornea and using a laser to reshape the underlying tissue. Contoura Vision is a similar procedure to LASIK that also uses advanced diagnostic technology to customize the treatment for each patient's unique vision requirements.

In general, SMILE may be a better option for people with thinner corneas, but it also depends on other factors such as the degree of refractive error, corneal irregularities, and the thickness of the corneal tissue in the treatment area. Contoura Vision, on the other hand, is more customizable, allowing for precise vision correction and improved night vision.

It is important to discuss both options in detail with your surgeon and get a thorough evaluation before making a decision. Your surgeon will be able to recommend the best option based on your individual needs and conditions.

Mad cow disease, also known as Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD), is a rare and serious brain condition that is caused by the accumulation of abnormal proteins in the brain. The disease can only be transmitted through the consumption of infected meat, not through touching or handling infected dog food.

In addition, the risk of contracting CJD from infected animal products is extremely low, as many countries have strict regulations and testing procedures in place to prevent the spread of the disease.

Therefore, it is highly unlikely that you have contracted CJD from handling the dog food or feeding the puppies. There is no need to blow your savings on unnecessary travel or worry about symptoms.

However, if you are experiencing symptoms such as dizziness, confusion, memory loss, or muscle weakness, it is important to seek medical attention immediately. It's also important to note that these symptoms can have many other causes besides CJD.

input: Hi doctor,I have nausea every time and every day after eating. I cannot eat my food. I drink only water. I am feeling tired always. My stomach hurts me so much. Why do I have a feeling of nausea after eating? Why do I throw up after eating?
answer_icliniq: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. I have gone through the attachment (attachment removed to protect patient identity). Your BMI (body mass index) is in borderline. It is normal. I can understand your concern regarding vomiting. It can occur due to various reasons. Gastritis can also be a reason for having nausea and vomiting. Take food at regular intervals and avoid skipping breakfast, lunch or dinner. Follow a diet schedule. Avoid eating oily, fried and spicy foods. Take plenty of oral fluids. You may also need to take antacid and antiemetic if it is required.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello! I'm sorry to hear about your symptoms. There are many possible reasons why you may be experiencing nausea and vomiting after eating. Here are some possible causes:

1. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD): This occurs when stomach acid backs up into the esophagus, causing irritation and inflammation. Symptoms include heartburn, nausea, and vomiting.

2. Food allergies or intolerances: Certain foods or drinks may cause an allergic reaction or intolerance, leading to nausea and vomiting. Examples include dairy, gluten, and soy.

3. Infection: A viral or bacterial infection in the digestive system can cause nausea and vomiting after eating.

4. Gastritis: This is inflammation of the stomach lining, which can be caused by an infection, excessive alcohol intake, or prolonged use of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs).

5. Peptic ulcer disease: An ulcer in the stomach or duodenum can cause nausea, vomiting, and stomach pain.

6. Gastroparesis: This is a condition where the stomach takes longer than normal to empty its contents into the small intestine. Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, and early satiety (feeling full after eating a small amount of food).

7. Pregnancy: Nausea and vomiting are common symptoms of pregnancy, especially in the first trimester.

To determine the cause of your symptoms, it's important to see a doctor for an evaluation. They may recommend tests such as a stomach acid test, endoscopy, or imaging studies. Treatment will depend on the underlying cause of your symptoms.

For now take Omeprazole twice daily for 10 days and avoid spicy and oily food. Take plenty of fluids to prevent dehydration. You can also take antacid like Pantoprazole before breakfast. Hope I have answered your query. Let me know if I can assist you further.

If your symptoms persist or worsen, it's important to seek medical attention as soon as possible.

Hello! Thank you for sharing your concerns. It is not uncommon to experience blushing and increased heart rate during social interactions, especially when meeting new people or in situations that make us feel uncomfortable or vulnerable. As you mentioned, this could be related to anxiety.

Assuming that your symptoms are primarily anxiety-related, there are several strategies and treatments that may be helpful in managing blushing and increased heart rate during social situations. Some options include:

1. Behavioral therapy: Learning to recognize and challenge negative thoughts that contribute to anxiety can be helpful in reducing symptoms. A therapist trained in cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) can work with you to develop coping strategies and techniques to manage social anxiety.

2. Medications: Your doctor may recommend medication to manage symptoms of anxiety. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) and benzodiazepines are commonly used to treat social anxiety disorder.

3. Relaxation techniques: Practicing relaxation techniques such as deep breathing or progressive muscle relaxation can help manage symptoms of anxiety during social interactions.

4. Exposure therapy: Gradually exposing yourself to social situations that trigger your anxiety can help you build confidence and reduce your symptoms over time.

It is important to remember that everyone experiences anxiety differently, and what works for one person may not work for another. I recommend discussing your symptoms with your physician or a mental health professional to determine the best course of treatment for you.

I hope this information helps, and I wish you all the best on your journey towards managing your symptoms.

Hello! Facet hypertrophy is a condition where the joints in your spine called facet joints become enlarged and may cause lower back pain. Here are some treatment options that you can consider:

1. Medications: Anti-inflammatory drugs like ibuprofen and acetaminophen can help in reducing pain and inflammation. Muscle relaxants like cyclobenzaprine can be helpful in relaxing tense muscles.

2. Physical therapy: A physical therapist can help in designing an exercise program that can improve your posture, flexibility, and strength of your back muscles. This can eventually reduce pain and improve your mobility.

3. Steroid injections: Corticosteroids can be injected into the facet joint to decrease inflammation or swelling.

4. Surgery: Surgery is usually the last resort and is recommended in severe cases where conservative methods have failed to reduce pain. The procedure may involve removing part of the facet joint to relieve pressure.

In addition to these treatments, practicing good posture, losing weight if you are overweight, and avoiding activities that aggravate your symptoms can be helpful in managing facet hypertrophy. I would advise you to consult your doctor who can guide you on the best treatment options based on your symptoms and medical history.

input: Hello doctor,My son is 6 years old. He got lung allergy so the doctor gave Levolin 100 and Budecort inhalers and some syrup like Montair LC kid. So my son is getting free from the fever, coughs, and cold by using these medicines. After completion of these medicines course, he is repeating with the same cough, cold and fever. Where as he will not eat any foods like chocolates and other flavored items, but he eats very rarely.On using these medicines is there any side effect in his growing future? I want to know is there any permanent solution for him without using these medicines and being healthy? And he is getting this problem when there are changes in weather and if he eats any chocolate and other foods. My son gets a fever with a high temperature of 101 and goes to 103 degree celcius. Sometimes we used to give him Meftal 8 ml and P-250 or Calpol for every four hours when the temperature was 100. After giving these medicines he was being sweated a lot specially on his head. Why there is sweating from head? Is this sweating from head cause any problem? How long should he use these mediciens? What are the foods that are good for him and can we give him dry fruits and fruits? Which are best for him?
answer_icliniq: Hello, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. The doctor has given the right treatment and please follow his instructions. The inhalers are safe and do not have any side effects as the dose of medicine is in micrograms which is very small. The allergic problem will resolve by itself as he grows. Sweating when fever reduces is very common and not a matter of concern. After the course of calcium and vitamin D3 sweating problem will also reduce. About the food discuss with your pediatrician as it depends on the individual child.  ChatDoctor.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello,

It sounds like your son is experiencing recurrent episodes of respiratory infections, likely due to his underlying lung allergy. The Levolin and Budecort inhalers are commonly prescribed for asthma or bronchospasm, which is a narrowing of the airways in response to allergens or other triggers. Montair LC kid is an antihistamine and leukotriene antagonist, which can help reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of allergies.

The use of these medications is generally considered safe and effective when used as directed by a physician. However, like all medications, they may have potential side effects such as increased heart rate, tremors, and irritability in some individuals. These side effects are usually mild and temporary, and are outweighed by the benefits of improved breathing and reduced symptoms.

To address the underlying cause of your son's lung allergy, it would be important to identify and avoid any triggers that may be contributing to his symptoms. Common allergens include dust mites, pet dander, pollen, mold, and certain foods. If you suspect that certain foods are exacerbating his symptoms, it may be helpful to keep a food diary and see if there is any correlation between his symptoms and specific foods. Avoiding exposure to tobacco smoke and other environmental irritants can also be helpful.

There is no single "cure" for lung allergy or asthma, but with proper management and avoidance of triggers, most individuals are able to maintain good control of their symptoms and lead healthy, active lives. Your son may need to continue using these medications for a longer duration, until his symptoms are well controlled and he can tolerate foods that previously triggered his symptoms.

Regarding the fever, it is a natural response to infection and helps the body fight off the offending agent. Use of medications like Meftal or Calpol can help reduce fever and improve comfort but should be used at the recommended dose and duration. It is also important to ensure good hydration during febrile illness.

Sweating on the head during fever is a normal response and is part of the body's thermoregulation. It does not usually cause any harm.

Regarding diet, a well-balanced, nutritious diet that includes plenty of fruits, vegetables, and whole grains can help support overall health and immunity. Dry fruits and nuts are also a good source of healthy fats, protein, and fiber. However, if your son has a known food allergy or intolerance, it may be necessary to avoid certain foods or consult with an allergist to determine if further evaluation is needed.

I hope this information is helpful. It is always a good idea to follow up with your son's pediatrician or a specialist if you have any concerns or questions about his health or treatment plan.

answer_chatgpt:
Hi! It sounds like you may have developed a condition called shin splints. It is common in dancers who perform activities that involve rapid and forceful movement of the feet and lower legs. It is important to address shin splints as soon as possible to prevent further injury.

Here are some suggestions:

1. Rest: Take a few days off from dancing to rest your shins. Avoid any activities that aggravate your condition.

2. Ice: Apply ice to your shins for 20 minutes at a time, three to four times a day.

3. Compression: Wear compression socks or sleeves to reduce swelling.

4. Elevation: Elevate your legs for 20 minutes, three to four times a day, to reduce swelling.

5. Modify your activities: Take a break from jumping and high-impact moves for a while. Also, avoid activities that require sudden starts and stops.

6. Stretching: Stretch your calves, quads, and hamstrings to improve flexibility and reduce tension.

7. Strengthening exercises: Do exercises that strengthen your lower legs and ankles.

If your symptoms persist, consider seeing a healthcare professional or a physical therapist to develop a personalized treatment plan.

Regarding switching to Pilates, it may be a good idea to complement your dance routine with low-impact activities like Pilates or yoga to avoid overuse injuries. However, it is important to address the underlying cause of your shin pain first before switching to a new exercise routine.

input: Hello doctor,What is raw water and how is it different from the tap, spring, and other types of water? What are the perceived benefits and dangers of drinking raw water? When did this fad start? Have you ever seen a patient getting sick by drinking raw water?
answer_icliniq: Hi. Raw water consumption has become quite a thing recently, particularly in certain parts of the united states and among people depending on or advocating alternative or herbal medicine. It is unprocessed and totally natural water so it is high in salt contents and other constituents that prevent or slow down cellular aging, however this is just theoretical with no published results. It differs from filtered water and spring water in its high salt contents and silica and lacking chlorine and fluoride which are usually found in processed water. Also, the main difference is that it contains many pathogenic organisms ranging from bacteria to parasites causing various diseases and serious infections some of which are life-threatening. Namely, typhoid and paratyphoid fever, leptospirosis, shigellosis, schistosomiasis, cholera, amoebiasis, etc. I have heard of many cases of raw water making people sick. Cases of typhoid, amoebiasis, and cholera which are water borne diseases are quite prevalent in some areas.
answer_chatgpt:
Raw water is a natural water source that has not undergone any treatment, such as filtration or disinfection, which occurs through the process of managing and distributing public water supply. In contrast, tap water, spring water, and other types of water typically undergo treatments to ensure they are safe for consumption.

The perceived benefits of drinking raw water are that it contains natural minerals and microbes that can be beneficial for gut health, immune function, and overall well-being. However, there are also potential dangers with drinking raw water, including the presence of harmful bacteria and parasites that can cause illness, such as E. coli, Giardia, and Cryptosporidium.

The fad of drinking raw water gained popularity in the United States around 2017 when companies started selling untreated water sourced from springs and other natural sources. However, various health organizations have cautioned against the dangers of drinking raw water, emphasizing the importance of water treatment and ensuring safe and clean water sources.

As a medical AI, I cannot say from personal experience whether I have seen patients getting sick from drinking raw water. Nevertheless, drinking untreated water can be risky and has led to numerous outbreaks of waterborne illnesses globally. Drinking untreated water can result in severe diarrhea, vomiting, and other gastrointestinal issues, leading to complications that can be life-threatening in some cases.
answer_chatdoctor: Hi there, Raw water is water that has not been treated in any way. It comes straight from its source - usually groundwater or surface water such as lakes and streams. Unlike treated water which goes through a process of filtration and chemical treatment to make it safe for human consumption, raw water does not undergo any of these processes. This means that it may contain bacteria, viruses, parasites and other organisms that can cause illness if consumed. The most common risks associated with consuming raw water are gastrointestinal illnesses like diarrhea, vomiting, and cramps. In rare cases, more serious diseases such as typhoid fever, Cholera, hepatitis A, and polio have also been reported. There are no health benefits associated with drinking raw water. The only benefit it offers is that it is natural and untreated. The practice of drinking raw water

input: Hello doctor,My wife is 70 years old and was diagnosed with typhoid and was given Ceftas 200 TDS for 21 days and Argex 500 OD for seven days. The Widal test showed typhi O: 1:160, typhi H: 1:160, typhi AH: 1:80 and typhi BH: 1:40 after 10 days of treatment. The treatment was continued as above and after 2 to 4 days the temperature came down to 98.0 to 98.8. Now five days after completing the medicine course we again got her Widal test and results are the same as were in firsts test. Now, I would like to be advised how to treat her weakness and is there any treatment to kill the bacteria causing the fever and should we go for vaccine inoculation once more now.
answer_icliniq: Hello. I guess, she is not having a fever now. If fever has settled down then it means all bacterias are killed. For more information consult a general medicine physician online
answer_chatgpt:
Hello, I'm an AI language model and I can provide some information related to your concern.

Typhoid fever is caused by the bacteria Salmonella typhi, which can infect the digestive system and spread through contaminated food or water. The recommended treatment for typhoid fever is antibiotics, such as Ceftas, which aim to kill the bacteria.

The Widal test measures the antibodies produced by the body in response to a Salmonella typhi infection. It is used to confirm a diagnosis of typhoid fever and to monitor the progress of treatment. In your wife's case, the Widal test showed high levels of antibodies ten days after starting treatment, which is expected. However, the test results after completing the treatment don't indicate any significant changes in antibody levels, which raises concern about the effectiveness of the antibiotics.

It's important to note that a Widal test alone is not sufficient to determine the success of treatment, and your wife's doctor may want to monitor other signs and symptoms, such as fever, for a few more days before deciding on further action. In some cases, a repeat course of antibiotics may be necessary if the initial treatment did not fully eradicate the infection.

Regarding your wife's weakness, typhoid fever can cause fatigue and weakness even after the infection is treated. It's essential to ensure that she continues to stay hydrated and eat a healthy diet to support her recovery. Additionally, some gentle exercises can improve her strength and energy levels.

Vaccination can help prevent typhoid fever, but it's not recommended as a treatment for an active infection. The vaccine may be recommended for people at high risk of exposure, such as those traveling to areas with endemic typhoid fever.

I recommend consulting your wife's doctor for further guidance on her treatment and recovery.
answer_chatdoctor: 1) She can continue taking ceftas for another 7-10 days.2) She needs to have a good healthy diet rich in vitamins and minerals, especially iron.3) Give her plenty of fluids so that she does not get dehydrated.4) Typhoid is a chronic disease and it will take some time to completely recover from it.5) Vaccination is recommended only if she has repeated episodes of typhoid.

input: Hello doctor,Can one be color blind if one has 20/20 vision?
answer_icliniq: Hello, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. Well, for your knowledge color blindness and color deficiency are two different things. You gain color vision due to cone cells in your retina. True color blinds see only black and white. They have no cones or have only blue cones. This is rare and they do not have 20/20 vision they nearly have 20/200. Most others are color deficient people; however, it is erroneously used as a color blind. They have either two types of cones including the blue and all three types of cones. They see the world colored but they have issues with color discrimination. They can have 20/20 vision. For more information consult an eye care ophthalmologist online
answer_chatgpt: Yes, it is possible to have 20/20 vision but also be color blind. These are two separate conditions that can coexist in an individual. 20/20 vision refers to the sharpness or clarity of a person's vision, while color blindness refers to the inability to distinguish certain colors or shades of color. Color blindness is typically caused by an inherited genetic mutation, and it affects both males and females.
answer_chatdoctor: 1. No, not at all, but it is possible to have normal vision and still be color blind. Color blindness is an inherited condition that affects the way certain colors appear to people with this condition. It is caused by problems in the cells of the retina that respond to light.  The cells are called cones. People who are color blind usually cannot distinguish shades of red, green or blue. Someone with this condition may also have difficulty seeing yellow, brown, and gray. Color blindness can be passed down from parents to their children.

To avoid getting pregnant, you could consider using emergency contraception such as the I-pill. It is best to take it as soon as possible, ideally within 72 hours (but can be effective for up to 5 days). You should take a single pill, and it is available over the counter at most pharmacies. 

In addition to emergency contraception, there are other preventive measures available such as regular contraceptive pills, condoms, or intrauterine devices (IUDs). These methods provide long-term protection and are more effective than emergency contraception.

To determine your safe period, you should track your menstrual cycle regularly. The safe period is considered to be the time in your cycle when you are least likely to get pregnant. It is usually calculated as the days leading up to and following ovulation, during which time you should avoid having unprotected intercourse.

It is recommended that you consult with a healthcare provider to discuss the most suitable contraceptive method for you and to receive further advice on how to manage your menstrual cycles.

input: Hello doctor,You were wonderful in allaying some health anxiety over some skin issues several months ago. So, I thought I would consult with you on another question pertaining to my son. Unfortunately, a combination of my health anxiety and the big mistake of searching online has my mind imagining the worst with regard to my son. My son is 19 years old and has no diagnosed medical issues. I noticed back when he was about eight or ten years old that he had a mark resembling a cafe au lait mark on his back, in the upper shoulder area. I had always asked the pediatrician to look at it during his yearly physicals, and they made nothing of it. Throughout the years it has grown a bit and has become darker and more rough in texture. It is probably slightly less than an inch in length and looks like a few small lesions grouped together. Again, I have brought it to the pediatrician\s attention and he was not concerned. Three months ago, I made an appointment for him and my other son with a dermatologist for a skin exam, and I pointed out the spots which I wanted to be checked in particular. We met with the physician\s assistant there, and my younger son had a peculiar spot I pointed out, of which the PA conducted a shave biopsy, which came back as a mildly atypical junctional nevus. For my older son, I pointed out the spot on his back/shoulder, and the PA diagnosed it as a linear epidermal nevus. She said the chances of it becoming dangerous are slim to none. It may darken throughout the years, and they will simply monitor it at yearly visits. Unfortunately, I went on to research online about epidermal nevi and of course I ran into information showing a possible link to epidermal nevus syndrome and all of its associated neurological and skeletal defects. As I said, he is 19 and has no medical issues, is extremely intelligent, etc. In your opinion, is it safe to say that I should not be concerned about those syndromes? I presume it is primarily related to the numerous and systemic epidermal nevi, which he does not have, and I would think it would have presented by now? And my last even more neurotic question. My online research led me to some information saying that a certain type of epidermal nevus could be related to a gene mutation that could be passed along to an offspring, with them having possible serious skin scaling conditions, etc. Again, he has just one solitary small epidermal nevus. No biopsy was done, as the PA did not feel it was necessary. Do you feel there is any need for me to worry about that potential genetic mutation? Thank you so much for reading my long query.
answer_icliniq: Hello, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. A solitary epidermal nevus is very common and does not pose any kind of serious health concern. Neither is a biopsy required. You are right. Any associated symptom should have been present since birth or early childhood in this case. Even if it is passed on to the future generations, it is harmless. A yearly medical examination is more than enough for the nevi in both your kids. Good luck.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello! I am happy to help address your concerns. Based on what you’ve described, it sounds like your son has a single linear epidermal nevus, which is a benign skin lesion. These are caused by a genetic mutation in the embryonic development of the skin and are not generally associated with any other medical conditions. While epidermal nevus syndrome can be associated with multiple and widespread epidermal nevi, your son’s single lesion does not suggest that he has this syndrome. Additionally, even if he did have epidermal nevus syndrome, the associated neurological and skeletal defects are not typically present until infancy or childhood, so it is unlikely that he would develop these conditions at his current age.

In terms of passing on a gene mutation to his offspring, the risk is generally low, especially if there is only one isolated lesion. While some epidermal nevi can be associated with specific gene mutations, this is not always the case and may not be relevant to your son’s condition. It is still a good idea to continue monitoring the lesion as recommended by his dermatologist, but there is no need to worry about him passing on any significant genetic risk to his offspring.

I hope this information helps to ease your concerns. Please let me know if you have any further questions or concerns.
answer_chatdoctor: Hi, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. I can understand your concern regarding your sons health problem. There is no need to worry about this issue. The epidermal nevus is benign condition which occurs due to abnormal pigmentation of the skin. It doesn't spread anywhere else in the body, and it won't cause any harmful effects. Epidermal nevi don't usually cause problems unless they change color, size or shape. But if you are worried about it then you can see a dermatologist and get evaluated. Hope you find this answer helpful. Wishing you good health. Take care.

answer_chatgpt:
Hello! I'm sorry to hear that your sister has chickenpox. You should definitely take measures to prevent getting the virus. Chickenpox is highly contagious and can easily be spread through contact with saliva, mucus, or blisters of an infected person. The virus can also be spread by airborne respiratory droplets from an infected person's coughing or sneezing.

To prevent getting chickenpox, you should:

1. Avoid close contact with your sister, especially when she is coughing or sneezing. Stay at least 6 feet away from her at all times, and use a mask and gloves when necessary.
2. Wash your hands frequently with soap and water, especially after coming into contact with your sister's belongings or surfaces she may have touched.
3. Get vaccinated if you haven't already had chickenpox. This will greatly reduce your risk of becoming infected or developing a severe case of the disease.
4. Boost your immune system by eating a healthy diet and getting adequate rest and exercise.

The chances of contracting chickenpox when living with an infected person are quite high without vaccination. If you do get infected with chickenpox, it typically takes about 10-21 days for the rash to scab over. It is important to note that you are contagious from one to two days before the rash appears until all the blisters have crusted over, which usually takes around five to ten days. The scabs generally take around one to three weeks to fall off completely.

It is understandable that you are feeling self-conscious about the scabs from the rash. You should take extra care during this time to avoid scratching the blisters, as this can lead to scarring. It is essential to keep the area clean and dry to ensure the blisters heal without infection.

I hope this helps! Stay safe and healthy.

If your symptoms persist or worsen, please consult with a healthcare professional for further evaluation and treatment.

answer_chatgpt:
Taking Dilantin (phenytoin) for a long period of time can cause unwanted side effects, even in people who don't have a history of seizure disorders. Some of the side effects include:

- Coordination problems
- Dizziness and drowsiness
- Confusion and memory problems
- Gum overgrowth
- Skin rash
- Nausea and vomiting
- Blurry vision
- Low blood pressure

In very rare cases, long-term use of phenytoin can lead to a condition called "drug-induced lupus," which can cause joint pain, rash, and fever.

If you've been taking phenytoin for several weeks by mistake, the best course of action is to stop taking it gradually under the supervision of a healthcare provider. Stopping it suddenly can cause seizures and other withdrawal symptoms.

Your healthcare provider may also recommend monitoring your blood levels of phenytoin to see if you need any dose adjustments or if the medication needs to be stopped completely.

Overall, it's best to consult with a healthcare provider as soon as possible to determine the best course of treatment for your specific situation.
answer_chatdoctor: 300 mg of dilantin is very high dose and can cause severe side effects like seizures, so it should be stopped immediately as soon as possible. You can take dimethyl carbamazepine instead which is less sedative than dilantin and safe at such high doses. It may take some time to stop the medication completely but you have to consult your doctor about the same.

Many a times, such mobility turns out to be more physiological than pathological (as it turned out for me).  Take care.

Thank you for your question. It is good that you are taking steps towards improving your dental hygiene. Based on your symptoms, it is important to ensure that the dentist has thoroughly examined your gums and teeth to rule out any potential underlying conditions. If your gums are red and appear decayed, it may be an indication of gingivitis or early-stage gum disease. Gingivitis is caused by plaque buildup on teeth, leading to inflammation of the gums. This can cause gums to become red, swollen, and tender, and may lead to tooth sensitivity.

If you are experiencing grinding, then it may also be contributing to your symptoms. Grinding, also known as bruxism, can wear down your teeth, cause them to loosen, and damage your gums over time. You may have a sore jaw or wake up with headaches as well.

The dentist can recommend solutions to help alleviate grinding and prevent further tooth and gum damage. This may include a nightguard or other splint to protect your teeth while you sleep. It is also important to address the underlying cause of grinding, which can be stress, anxiety, or sleep disorders.

Overall, I recommend following up with your dentist to discuss your concerns and get a thorough examination of your gums and teeth. They can provide personalized recommendations to improve your oral health and address any potential issues.

input: Hello doctor,My husband has a heart condition that means his arteries build up a plaque at a much higher rate than normal. I know that meat is not good for the heart, so we limit it and always go for lean cuts, but I am confused about the difference between animal-based saturated fats and plant-based saturated fats.For example, everyone raves about the health benefits of coconut oil and coconut milk, but both are extremely high in saturated fat. I googled this and find reports stating that it is a different kind of saturated fat and does not affect the heart badly, but I do not know if this is true. For someone like my husband, is all saturated fat bad? Also, is chicken really healthier meat than beef? It seems to have a high cholesterol content so I do not know if he should be eating it. Any advice would be much appreciated.He is currently Rosuvastatin, Bystolic, and Aspirin.

Just to make it easy, we need some fat which is good and HDL (high-density lipoprotein) like almonds and walnuts are good as these are rich in omega 3 and 6 fatty acids. Similarly, the fat we have in coconut oil is different. The fat in the chicken can be removed by removing the skin, where as beef has inbuilt fat which cannot be removed easily hence we suggest to include fish and chicken avoiding beef. Considering fat, we miss out on other nutrients too, so it is advised to reduce the oil intake like healthy individuals can take half a liter of oil per month if you are two your cooking should be within 1 liter of oil only. Coconut oil is having saturated fat but it burns easily in high humidity areas. If we live in high humidity area it is better to include coconut oil to protect our joints and skin. If we leave apart from high humidity area it is better to use refined oil.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello!

You have a valid concern about your husband's diet, and I can definitely help you with that.

To answer your question, all saturated fats are not created equal. The type of saturated fat found in animal products like beef and pork has been shown to increase levels of LDL cholesterol, the "bad" cholesterol that can build up in arteries and increase the risk of heart disease.

On the other hand, plant-based saturated fats like those found in coconut oil, palm oil, and cocoa butter may not have the same negative effects on heart health. However, it's important to note that while these fats may not increase LDL cholesterol, they can still be calorie-dense and potentially contribute to weight gain or other health issues if consumed in excess.

In terms of protein sources, chicken can be a healthier choice than beef if you choose lean cuts and remove the skin. However, it's still important to limit intake and balance protein sources with plant-based options like legumes, lentils, nuts, and seeds.

Overall, it's important for your husband to maintain a low-fat and low-cholesterol diet, and to focus on consuming plenty of whole, unprocessed foods like fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean proteins.

I hope this helps, and if you have any other questions or concerns, don't hesitate to ask!
answer_chatdoctor: 1) Coconut oil is beneficial as it contains medium chain triglycerides (MCT). These are easily digested by the body and are converted into energy. input: Hi doctor,I disk golf in an area where a decent amount of poison oak is present. I would like to know how fast urushiol binds to skin, and once it does is there any possibility to spread?I use an electronic cigarette which requires me to handle cotton that I feed into the atomizer. My doubt is if urushiol vaporizes automatically, what will be the harm?
answer_icliniq: Hello, Welcome to Chat Doctor forum. I have read your query. Poison oak causes allergic contact dermatitis (ACD) and is the most common cause of ACD than all the other causes combined. The allergen which cause ACD is usually present within the sap named urushiol and it will be mainly present in the leaves and the inner stems of the plant. After the contact with any part of the plant it usually takes 12 - 48 hours for the symptoms to occur. But the resin will bond immediately to the skin on contact. So, you should remove it with a mild soap wash immediately. If within 10 - 15 minutes of contact washing is done, 50% can be removed. But, if it is beyond one hour, washing does not help much. You can use Bentonite barrier creams which can prevent bonding of urushiol to your exposed parts. Urushiol once bound to the skin does not vaporize and does not spread to other parts of the body. As it is a local reaction, the intensity of it depends on the amount of resin contacted.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello! Urushiol, which is the oil found in poison oak, binds to the skin immediately upon contact. This happens quickly, often within 10-15 minutes of contact with the plant. If the oil is not washed off the skin thoroughly with soap and water, it can continue to spread and cause a reaction for up to 5 days. 

As for handling cotton that is used in an e-cigarette, there is very little chance of the urushiol vaporizing and causing harm. Urushiol requires physical contact with the skin in order to cause an allergic reaction, and it does not naturally vaporize. However, if there is oil from the poison oak on your hands and you touch the cotton, the oil can be transferred to the cotton and potentially cause a reaction if it comes into contact with your skin. It is important to thoroughly wash your hands after coming into contact with poison oak to prevent the spread of the oil.
answer_chatdoctor: 1) Poison oak / ivy has oil called Urushiol which can cause allergic reaction when comes in contact with skin or mucous membrane2) It can take few hours for Urushiol to come in contact with skin and then cause allergy3) Once Urushiol contacts skin, it takes time for it to leave the body. It might stay on skin for up to 5 days4) Yes, Urushiol can get vaporized and can cause allergic reaction if inhaled. Hope I have answered your query. Let me know if I can assist you further.

input: Hello doctor,In my left kidney, there are multiple stones. The largest stone is 26 mm. And I have got my kidneys operated two times. First was an open surgery six years back and the second was endoscopy three years back. Can the 26 mm stone be removed by any medicine?
answer_icliniq: Hi. Cystone and Calcuri (Ayurvedic tablets) can be taken. Two tablets morning and two tablets evening 5 PM can be taken continuously for three months. Then you may repeat the scan. This size is difficult to dissolve within a short period, takes a long time. If you have pain, you may go for lithotripsy surgery and do the chemical analysis of the stone. This will give the type of stones. Based on this we can plan herbal therapy.
answer_chatgpt:
Hello,

A 26 mm kidney stone is a significant size and is unlikely to be passed naturally or dissolved by medication. It would be best to consult with a urologist who will assess the stone and advise on the most suitable management plan.

Depending on the location and composition of the stone, treatment options may include:

1. Shock wave lithotripsy (SWL): In this non-invasive procedure, high-energy shock waves are directed towards the stone from outside the body to break it into smaller fragments.

2. Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL): This surgery is recommended for larger stones and involves making a small incision in the back to insert a scope and remove the stone.

3. Ureteroscopy and laser lithotripsy: In this minimally invasive procedure, a small scope is inserted through the urethra and bladder to the ureter, and a laser is used to break up the stone.

It is important to follow up with your urologist regularly and maintain a healthy lifestyle to prevent further stone formation.
